stem: An associate needs Claude to draft a product-announcement email to existing customers. Which prompt is most likely to produce a usable first draft on the first attempt?
options: Write a friendly product-announcement email that customers will like.
Draft an email announcing our latest product update to the mailing list.
Write a 150-word announcement email to existing customers introducing our new reporting dashboard, in a warm, professional tone, ending with a single call to action to book a demo.
Write a product announcement about the new dashboard and make it engaging.
rationale: Effective prompts specify task, audience, length, tone, format, and desired outcome. Option 3 supplies all of these, so Claude has an unambiguous target. Options 1, 2, and 4 name the general task but omit audience, length, or the intended action, leaving Claude to guess and producing an unfocused draft.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: A request requires analyzing 200 open-ended survey responses, identifying the main themes, and writing an executive summary. What is the most reliable way to structure the work with Claude?
options: Ask Claude to extract and list the themes first, review that list, then have it write the summary based on the confirmed themes.
Paste all responses and ask for the executive summary in a single step, then lightly edit the result.
Ask Claude to write the summary first and infer the themes from it afterward.
Split the responses across several unrelated chats and merge whatever summaries come back.
rationale: Task decomposition into ordered, checkable steps (extract themes, verify, then summarize) lets the associate validate intermediate output and improves final quality. Option 2 skips verification; option 3 inverts the logical order so themes are reverse-engineered from an unchecked summary; option 4 fragments the data and loses the shared context needed for coherent themes.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompting best practices

stem: Claude's first draft of an internal update is accurate but too formal for a casual team channel. What is the most efficient next step?
options: Rewrite the update manually from scratch to control the tone.
Ask Claude to revise the existing draft into a more casual, conversational tone.
Start a new conversation and re-enter the original request unchanged.
Post the formal version, since tone is a matter of personal preference.
rationale: Iterating on the existing draft preserves the accurate content and fixes only the tone, which is the fastest path. Rewriting from scratch discards good work; a fresh identical prompt is unlikely to change the tone; and posting the mismatched version ignores a solvable audience-fit problem.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompting best practices

stem: Which techniques generally improve prompt quality for business tasks? (Select THREE.)
options: Provide relevant background context and explicit constraints.
Specify the desired output format and length.
Include a short example of the style or structure you want.
Keep instructions broad so Claude can interpret the task freely.
Rely on evaluative adjectives such as 'make it compelling' as the primary instruction.
rationale: Context and constraints, explicit format and length, and a concrete example all give Claude a clearer target and more consistent output. Keeping instructions broad increases variability rather than quality, and vague evaluative adjectives without concrete direction give Claude nothing measurable to aim at.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompting best practices

stem: An associate wants Claude to help generate options for a new employee-wellness program. Which approach best fits an ideation task?
options: Ask Claude for the single best program it can recommend, then implement it.
Ask Claude for a wide range of distinct options first, then evaluate and narrow them in a follow-up prompt.
Ask Claude to produce one detailed option and expand only that one.
Provide the preferred conclusion and ask Claude to justify it.
rationale: Ideation benefits from divergent generation (many varied options) followed by convergence in a later step. Asking for a single recommendation or expanding one option skips the divergent phase and narrows too early; providing the conclusion and asking for justification biases the output toward a predetermined answer rather than exploring alternatives.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompting best practices

stem: An associate needs Claude to work through a multi-step logistics allocation problem where the reasoning matters as much as the answer. Which instruction best supports a reliable result?
options: Provide only the final allocation, with no explanation.
Work through the problem step by step, showing the reasoning, then state the final allocation.
Give the most likely allocation immediately and refine only if asked.
Prioritize a fast response over showing intermediate steps.
rationale: Asking Claude to reason step by step before the final answer improves accuracy on multi-step problems and makes the logic auditable. Suppressing the reasoning (options 1, 3, and 4) removes the associate's ability to catch a faulty step and tends to lower accuracy on chained calculations.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompting best practices

stem: Which instruction most clearly specifies the output format Claude should produce?
options: Give me a well-organized summary of Q3 sales.
Summarize Q3 sales as exactly three bullet points, each under 15 words.
Provide a clear and concise Q3 sales summary.
Summarize Q3 sales in a professional format.
rationale: Option 2 defines an unambiguous structure (three bullets, each under 15 words) that Claude can reproduce consistently. 'Well-organized,' 'clear and concise,' and 'professional format' are subjective descriptors that leave the actual structure undefined.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: An associate is shifting Claude from drafting marketing copy to producing a factual research brief for a regulated market. Which change in prompting priorities is most appropriate?
options: Emphasize vivid, persuasive language to keep the brief engaging.
Emphasize sourcing and scope, and instruct Claude to flag uncertainty rather than fill gaps.
Remove context to keep the prompt short and reduce the chance of bias.
Request the longest possible response to ensure completeness.
rationale: Research for a regulated market prioritizes accuracy, defined scope, and transparency about what is and isn't known, so instructing Claude to source claims and flag uncertainty is the key adaptation. Persuasive language suits marketing, not factual briefs; removing context degrades relevance rather than reducing bias; and maximizing length invites padding and unsupported claims.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Reduce hallucinations

stem: Claude's outputs are consistently longer than the associate needs. Which prompt change most directly reduces the length?
options: Add 'be thorough and complete' to the prompt.
State an explicit limit, such as 'respond in no more than 100 words.'
Ask Claude to add a detailed introduction before the main points.
Ask Claude to cover every possible angle of the topic.
rationale: An explicit word or length limit directly controls verbosity. The other three options ('be thorough,' add an introduction, cover every angle) all push the output longer, which is the opposite of the goal and a common mistake.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: Providing Claude with two worked examples that show the exact input-to-output pattern you want is best described as:
options: Zero-shot prompting
Few-shot (multishot) prompting
Fine-tuning the model
Chain-of-thought prompting
rationale: Supplying a small number of demonstration examples is few-shot (multishot) prompting. Zero-shot uses no examples; fine-tuning retrains model weights rather than adding examples in the prompt; chain-of-thought asks the model to show reasoning steps, which is unrelated to providing examples.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Use examples (multishot prompting)

stem: An associate's prompt says 'do not write anything unprofessional,' but the tone of outputs varies. Which revision is most likely to produce consistent tone?
options: Repeat the 'do not be unprofessional' instruction and add further prohibitions.
Describe the desired behavior directly: 'maintain a professional, courteous tone throughout.'
List several additional examples of unprofessional phrasing to avoid.
Leave the tone unspecified so Claude can adapt to each request.
rationale: Positive instructions that state the behavior you want are more reliable than stacking negative 'do not' constraints, which leave the target underdefined. Repeating prohibitions or listing what to avoid still describes tone only by exclusion, and leaving tone unspecified maximizes variability.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: What is the most accurate reason to give Claude a role such as 'act as an onboarding specialist'?
options: It guarantees the output will be factually accurate.
It sets a perspective, vocabulary, and level of detail suited to the task.
It removes the need to review the output before use.
It reduces the model's response time.
rationale: A role primes an appropriate perspective, vocabulary, and depth for the task. It does not guarantee factual accuracy, does not eliminate the need for human review, and has no meaningful effect on response speed.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompting best practices

stem: An associate must extract the same set of fields from hundreds of invoices. Which prompting approach scales best while keeping results consistent?
options: Reword the extraction request slightly for each invoice to avoid repetition.
Use one reusable template that names the required fields and fixes the output format.
Let Claude decide which fields to return for each invoice.
Submit each invoice without field instructions to keep the process flexible.
rationale: A reusable template with named fields and a fixed output format yields consistent, comparable results across many documents. Rewording each time, letting the model choose fields, or omitting field instructions all introduce variation that undermines consistency and downstream use.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompting best practices

stem: Claude returned an off-target draft that missed the intended audience and ran too long. Which two adjustments are the most reasonable first iterations? (Select TWO.)
options: Add the missing context about the audience and the goal.
Escalate the task to a developer to build a custom tool.
Specify the required output format and a length limit.
Abandon Claude and complete the draft manually.
Resend the same prompt without changes to get a second attempt.
rationale: Supplying the missing audience/goal context and specifying format and length are low-cost prompt iterations that directly address the two observed problems. Escalating to a developer or abandoning the tool is premature before basic iteration, and resending an unchanged prompt is unlikely to fix a targeting problem.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompting best practices

stem: Which statement best describes task decomposition as a prompting practice?
options: Combining several requests into one long prompt to save time.
Breaking a complex goal into smaller, ordered sub-tasks that can each be checked.
Removing constraints from a prompt to give the model more freedom.
Letting the model choose which parts of a task to complete.
rationale: Task decomposition means splitting a complex goal into smaller, sequential, verifiable steps, which improves reliability and reviewability. Option 1 describes the opposite (bundling); options 3 and 4 describe reducing control, not structured decomposition.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompting best practices

stem: Claude drafts a summary of a new regulation and cites a specific subsection number. The summary will go to the compliance team. What should the associate do first?
options: Send it as written, since the citation is specific and the tone is confident.
Verify the cited subsection against the official regulation text before sharing.
Ask Claude to rate its confidence in the citation and send it if the rating is high.
Reformat the summary to look more authoritative, then forward it.
rationale: Models can fabricate specific-looking details such as citation numbers, so factual claims must be checked against an authoritative source, especially for a compliance audience. Specificity and confident tone are not evidence of accuracy, self-reported confidence is not a reliable signal, and reformatting does nothing to confirm the underlying fact.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Reduce hallucinations

stem: An output states 'adoption rose 47% in Q2' but gives no source. The associate wants to include it in a board deck. The best next step is to:
options: Include it as-is, since the precise figure signals it is well grounded.
Treat it as unverified and confirm it against the underlying data before use.
Round it to 50% so the claim is easier to defend.
Keep it only because it strengthens the narrative of the deck.
rationale: Specificity is not evidence of accuracy; an unsourced statistic can be hallucinated and must be validated against the real data before use in a high-stakes deck. Trusting it for its precision, rounding it, or including it because it helps the story all bypass verification.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Reduce hallucinations

stem: In the context of using Claude for business tasks, which best defines a hallucination?
options: An answer the reader personally disagrees with.
Plausible-sounding content that is fabricated or unsupported by real sources.
A response that is shorter than the reader expected.
A response containing minor grammatical errors.
rationale: A hallucination is confident, plausible-sounding output that is not grounded in fact, such as invented citations, quotes, or statistics. Disagreement, brevity, and grammatical errors are unrelated to whether the content is factually grounded.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Reduce hallucinations

stem: A single report from Claude states a total of 1,200 units in one section and 1,020 units in another. This discrepancy is best characterized as:
options: Bias in the underlying training data.
An internal inconsistency to reconcile against the source before use.
A stylistic formatting choice with no impact on accuracy.
A fabricated citation.
rationale: Two conflicting figures within one output is an internal inconsistency that must be reconciled against the source data before the report is relied upon. It is not evidence of training bias, it is not a formatting choice, and it is not a citation problem since no source reference is involved.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Reduce hallucinations

stem: Across several deliverables, which factor most strongly signals that human verification is required before an output is used?
options: The output is written in complete, well-structured sentences.
The output will drive a high-stakes decision or reach an external or regulated audience.
The output is longer than a single paragraph.
The output is formatted with headings and bullet points.
rationale: The need for human review scales with the stakes and the audience: high-stakes, external, or regulated outputs require verification regardless of how polished they look. Sentence quality, length, and formatting are surface features that say nothing about the consequences of an error.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Reduce hallucinations

stem: An associate is validating a market-analysis brief that Claude produced. Which of the following are sound fact-checking and validation techniques? (Select THREE.)
options: Cross-check the key claims against authoritative primary sources
Reconcile the reported figures against the underlying dataset
Have a subject-matter expert review the high-stakes conclusions
Treat a claim as verified once Claude restates it consistently across regenerations
Accept figures reported to several decimal places as already validated
rationale: Cross-checking primary sources, reconciling figures against source data, and expert review of high-stakes conclusions are genuine validation techniques. Consistency across regenerations only shows the model is stable, not correct, and numeric precision is a formatting property, not evidence of accuracy.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: A candidate-screening summary generated by Claude characterizes applicants using inferences about their gender and age. Before the hiring team relies on it, the associate should:
options: Retain the summary but append a disclaimer noting it may contain bias
Remove the biased assumptions and regenerate using neutral, job-relevant criteria before relying on it
Pass it along as a first pass and let the hiring manager mentally discount any bias
Keep the demographic inferences on the theory that they may correlate with performance
rationale: Outputs can reflect and amplify bias, so the associate must remove the discriminatory inferences and re-run against neutral, job-relevant criteria. A disclaimer, informal 'discounting,' or retaining demographic inferences all leave biased content driving a consequential decision.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: What is the primary reason an associate would generate and compare two Claude responses to the same request?
options: To produce a longer combined answer
To assess which response better meets the task's accuracy, completeness, and audience needs
To demonstrate that the model is non-deterministic
To avoid closely reviewing either version
rationale: Comparing outputs lets the associate select or synthesize the version that best satisfies accuracy, completeness, and audience requirements. Length, proving non-determinism, and skipping review are not the purpose of comparison.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: An associate must adapt a detailed technical analysis for a non-technical executive audience. The most appropriate action is to:
options: Forward the technical version unchanged to save time
Rewrite it in plain language and lead with the business implications while preserving the underlying facts
Add domain jargon to signal analytical rigor to the executive
Reduce it to a single sentence regardless of what detail is lost
rationale: Audience adaptation means translating technical detail into plain language and foregrounding the implications that matter to that reader, without altering the facts. Sending it unchanged, adding jargon, or over-compressing all fail the audience's needs.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: When is a structured-data format such as a table the better choice than inline prose for presenting an output?
options: When the reader needs to scan, compare, or reuse repetitive or multi-attribute information
Whenever the response runs longer than a few sentences
When the goal is to convey a single narrative conclusion
When the underlying data is uncertain and should be de-emphasized
rationale: Tables suit comparative, repetitive, or multi-attribute information that readers scan or reuse; format should match how the information will be consumed. Length alone, single-narrative points, and de-emphasizing uncertain data are not the deciding factors.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: An Artifact is the most appropriate output format when the associate wants to:
options: Ask a quick factual question
Produce a substantial, reusable deliverable the user will iterate on and keep
Get a one-line conversational reply
Run a short calculation inline
rationale: Artifacts are designed for substantial, standalone deliverables meant to be edited, reused, or shared. Quick questions, one-line replies, and inline calculations belong in normal chat.
referenceText: Claude Help Center - What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: Claude confidently answers a question about an event from last week that likely falls after its knowledge cutoff. The safest interpretation is that:
options: The answer is reliable because it is stated confidently
The answer may be outdated or unverified and should be confirmed against a current source
Recent-news questions do not require additional verification
The specificity of the answer indicates that it is current
rationale: Time-sensitive claims can fall outside a model's reliable knowledge, so recent facts must be confirmed against current sources. Confidence, specificity, and assuming news needs no checking are all misleading signals.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: Which observation most clearly indicates that an output is incomplete rather than inaccurate?
options: It cites a source that cannot be located
It addresses only some parts of a multi-part request, omitting required sections
It states a figure that conflicts with the source data
It uses language that reflects demographic bias
rationale: Completeness concerns coverage: omitting required parts of a multi-part request is an incompleteness problem. A missing or false citation is an accuracy issue, a conflicting figure is an accuracy issue, and biased language is a fairness issue.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: Which outputs warrant heightened validation before they are used? (Select THREE.)
options: A legal interpretation that clients will rely on
A medication dosage recommendation for a patient
Financial figures being entered into an audited report
A brainstorm of team lunch options for an offsite
Ice-breaker questions for an internal workshop
rationale: Legal, medical, and financial outputs carry high consequences and require rigorous validation. A lunch brainstorm and workshop ice-breakers are low-stakes and do not demand the same scrutiny.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: An associate is curating research generated by Claude into an executive briefing. The best approach is to:
options: Include Claude's full output verbatim so that nothing is lost
Select and verify the relevant points, organize them, and discard unverified or off-topic material
Retain the longest section as the core of the briefing
Preserve conflicting claims side by side without noting the conflict
rationale: Curation means selecting, verifying, and organizing the relevant and accurate points, not dumping raw output. Verbatim inclusion, favoring length, and leaving contradictions unaddressed all undermine the briefing's reliability.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: Claude's answer includes a quoted 'expert statement' attributed to a named authority, but the associate cannot locate that person or quote in any source. This is best described as:
options: A properly sourced citation
A fabricated attribution that must not be presented as real
An acceptable paraphrase of general knowledge
A minor stylistic flourish
rationale: An unverifiable quote attributed to a named source is a fabricated attribution; presenting it as genuine would spread misinformation. It is neither a valid citation, a paraphrase, nor a cosmetic detail.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: What is the most reliable way to judge whether an output is factually accurate?
options: Ask Claude to confirm that it is certain of the answer
Check the specific claims against authoritative external evidence
Confirm the response is well written and grammatically correct
Count how many sources the response names
rationale: Accuracy is established by checking claims against authoritative evidence. Self-assessment, grammatical polish, and the number of sources cited are not reliable indicators of correctness.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: An associate wants to minimize the chance of publishing a hallucinated fact in a customer-facing FAQ. Which control is most effective?
options: Instruct Claude to be accurate and rely on that instruction
Require human verification of each factual claim against source material before publishing
Regenerate each answer several times and publish the most common version
Lengthen each answer so that more context is provided
rationale: A human verification step against source material is the effective control for factual claims in published content. Prompt instructions do not guarantee accuracy, majority-vote regeneration can consistently repeat the same error, and added length does not improve factual grounding.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: When Claude declines to answer or states that it is unsure, the associate should interpret this as:
options: A malfunction that should be overridden
A useful signal that the request may need verification, rephrasing, or human expertise
Grounds to resend an earlier, more confident response
Evidence that the tool is unsuitable for the task
rationale: Expressed uncertainty is informative and can indicate a need to verify, rephrase, or involve a human expert. Overriding it, reverting to a confidently wrong answer, or abandoning the tool all discard that signal.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: Which output is most appropriately delivered as inline chat text rather than as a saved Artifact?
options: A 12-page onboarding handbook
A brief clarification of one policy term the user asked about conversationally
A reusable project-charter template
A marketing plan that will be edited over several days
rationale: A short conversational clarification belongs inline. Handbooks, reusable templates, and iteratively edited plans are substantial deliverables better suited to Artifacts.
referenceText: Claude Help Center - What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: An associate receives a fluent, well-structured answer to a niche technical question. What is the correct takeaway about the answer's fluency?
options: Fluent, well-structured writing indicates the content is accurate
Fluency and structure are independent of accuracy, so the facts still require verification
A polished answer is unlikely to contain factual errors
Clear organization confirms that the answer is complete
rationale: Polished, well-organized writing is not evidence of correctness or completeness; the facts must still be verified. The other options mistake surface fluency for substantive accuracy or coverage.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: Two associates receive different answers to the same factual question from Claude. What is the best response?
options: Adopt the more detailed of the two answers
Investigate the discrepancy and confirm the correct answer against an authoritative source
Combine the two answers into an averaged response
Default to whichever answer was produced first
rationale: Divergent answers signal uncertainty that should be resolved against an authoritative source. Choosing by detail, averaging, or defaulting to the first response does not establish which answer is correct.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: An output will be translated and sent to customers across several countries. Which validation concern is added by this multi-locale distribution?
options: Confirming the email's visual formatting renders in each language
Verifying that claims, tone, and any regulated statements remain accurate and permissible in each jurisdiction
Ensuring the translated text preserves the original word count
Checking that the sender address is localized for each region
rationale: Cross-locale delivery adds substantive validation: claims and any regulated statements must remain accurate and appropriate for each audience and jurisdiction. Formatting, word count, and sender address are operational details, not the core validation risk.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: Which practices help an associate evaluate the completeness of an output? (Select TWO.)
options: Map the output back to each part of the original request
Confirm that every required section or field is present
Count the number of descriptive adjectives used
Treat a confident tone as evidence of full coverage
rationale: Completeness is assessed by mapping the output to every part of the request and confirming all required elements are present. Adjective counts and tone reveal nothing about coverage.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: An associate is unsure whether a Claude-generated summary faithfully reflects a 40-page source document. Which check is most reliable?
options: Re-run the summary at a shorter length and compare the two versions
Spot-check the summary's key claims and figures against the corresponding source passages
Confirm the summary preserves the document's section ordering
Check that the summary's length is proportional to the source
rationale: Verifying fidelity means spot-checking the summary's key claims and figures against the actual source passages to confirm nothing was distorted or invented. Comparing summary lengths, preserving section order, and proportional length do not test factual faithfulness.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 2: Output Evaluation and Validation

stem: An associate must generate a high volume of short customer-reply drafts each day, where turnaround speed and per-request cost matter far more than deep reasoning. Which model choice best fits this workload?
options: Route every reply through the most capable model to guarantee the highest possible quality.
Select a faster, lower-cost tier suited to straightforward, high-volume work.
Use the most capable model but shorten each prompt to reduce cost.
Alternate between model tiers at random to spread the load.
rationale: Matching the model to the task means using a faster, lower-cost tier (e.g., Haiku) for straightforward, high-volume work; the top tier is reserved for complex reasoning. Using the most capable model everywhere wastes cost and latency, shortening prompts does not change the tier's cost profile meaningfully, and random alternation ignores task fit.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Models overview

stem: A task requires deep, multi-step reasoning over a complex legal analysis where accuracy and quality are paramount and volume is low. Which model tier is best aligned?
options: The lowest-cost, fastest tier, to minimize latency.
A mid-tier balanced model to split the difference on cost.
The most capable tier, accepting higher latency and cost for quality.
Whichever tier currently has the most remaining usage credits.
rationale: High-stakes, complex reasoning justifies the most capable tier (e.g., Opus) even at higher cost and latency. The fastest tier trades away the quality this task demands, a balanced tier under-serves a quality-critical low-volume task, and remaining credits are not a capability criterion.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Models overview

stem: For capacity planning, which statement best characterizes the trade-offs across Claude's model tiers?
options: Haiku maximizes capability while Opus minimizes latency, and Sonnet is image-only.
Haiku prioritizes speed and low cost, Opus prioritizes capability for complex tasks, and Sonnet balances the two.
Opus and Haiku are both tuned for cost, while Sonnet is the only reasoning-capable tier.
The tiers differ only in context-window size, not in capability or cost.
rationale: As a planning heuristic, Haiku emphasizes speed/low cost, Opus emphasizes capability, and Sonnet balances performance and cost. The other options invert these roles or falsely claim the tiers differ only by context window.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Models overview

stem: An associate runs a recurring initiative and wants every related chat to start from the same instructions, files, and background context without setting it up each time. Which approach fits best?
options: Keep one very long conversation open and scroll back to reuse earlier context.
Maintain a personal document of prompts to paste at the start of each new chat.
Create a Claude Project that stores shared instructions and reference files for every related chat.
Use a separate account for the initiative to isolate its context.
rationale: Projects hold shared instructions and knowledge so every conversation in them starts with consistent context. A single long chat becomes unwieldy, manual pasting is error-prone and not persistent, and separate accounts do not share context across chats.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: An associate needs Claude to gather information from many sources and synthesize it into a thorough, cited report. Which capability is most fitting?
options: Ask a single, tightly scoped question and accept the first response.
Upload one authoritative source and summarize only that.
Use Research, which investigates multiple sources before synthesizing a cited report.
Split the work into many short, unrelated chats and merge the answers by hand.
rationale: Research is designed to investigate across multiple sources and synthesize the findings. A single scoped question or one source cannot deliver breadth, and manually merging disconnected chats is inefficient and loses coherence.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Research on Claude

stem: A conversation has grown very long and Claude begins to lose track of details established earlier in the thread. What is the best first action?
options: Continue adding messages, since the model re-reads the entire thread each turn.
Summarize the essential context and continue, or start a fresh chat seeded with that summary.
Switch to a higher-cost model, which retains longer memory automatically.
Clear the conversation and rely on the model recalling it from an earlier session.
rationale: When a thread outgrows the context window, condensing the key context and continuing (or restarting seeded with that summary) restores focus. Adding more messages worsens overflow, a higher-cost tier does not solve context limits, and the model does not recall cleared prior sessions.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: In which situation is an Artifact the more appropriate choice than a plain chat response?
options: When asking a brief factual question that needs a one-line answer.
When the output should be excluded from the conversation history.
When producing a substantial document or interactive piece the associate will iterate on and reuse.
When trying to reduce the model's token usage on a short reply.
rationale: Artifacts are built for substantial, reusable, iteratively edited deliverables. Brief questions belong inline, Artifacts do not hide output from history, and they are not a token-reduction mechanism.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: An associate needs Claude to draft internal meeting notes from a transcript, a routine, well-structured task. Which model choice is most cost-effective while still meeting the need?
options: The most capable, highest-cost tier, applied uniformly to every task.
A faster, lower-cost tier, which handles routine summarization reliably.
The tier with the largest context window, regardless of task complexity.
The most recently released tier, regardless of task fit.
rationale: Routine summarization is well served by a faster, lower-cost tier; reserving the top tier for it wastes budget. Context-window size and release recency are not the deciding factors for a routine summarization task.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Models overview

stem: An associate is choosing a model for a new workload. Which factors should drive that selection? (Select THREE.)
options: Required reasoning depth and output quality
Latency and throughput requirements
Cost and expected request volume
The model's internal release codename
The number of prior chats in the workspace
rationale: Model selection is driven by required reasoning depth/quality, latency/throughput needs, and cost/volume constraints. A release codename and the count of prior chats have no bearing on which tier fits the task.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Models overview

stem: Which statement most accurately describes how context works within a Claude conversation?
options: Claude permanently remembers every prior conversation and reuses it automatically.
Context is effectively unlimited; length affects only response speed.
Each conversation has a finite context window, so very long threads may need summarizing or restarting.
Only uploaded images consume the context window; text does not.
rationale: A conversation has a finite context window, and managing it by summarizing or restarting is a core skill for long threads. Claude does not automatically carry all past chats, context is not unlimited, and both text and images consume the window.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: An associate needs the same set of reference documents available across many related chats without re-uploading them each time. What is the best approach?
options: Paste the full documents into the first message of each new chat.
Add the documents as knowledge in a Project so every chat in it can draw on them.
Store the documents as Artifacts and link to them from each conversation.
Keep the documents in one long-running chat and branch from it each time.
rationale: Project knowledge makes reference material available to all conversations in the Project, eliminating re-uploads. Repasting is manual and error-prone, Artifacts are deliverables rather than a shared knowledge base, and a single branching chat does not scale across many related conversations.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: For a workload where quality, speed, and cost all matter but none clearly dominates, what is the most reasonable default model strategy?
options: Default to the cheapest tier and keep it regardless of results.
Default to the most capable tier for consistency across all tasks.
Start with a balanced tier and adjust up or down only if results or costs require it.
Run every task on two tiers in parallel and compare the outputs each time.
rationale: When no single factor dominates, a balanced tier is a sensible default, with escalation or downgrade based on observed results and cost. Fixed cheapest/most-capable defaults ignore the trade-off, and always running two tiers in parallel doubles cost unnecessarily.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Models overview

stem: An associate wants Claude's output to persist as an editable canvas they can revise over multiple sessions. Which option is most appropriate?
options: Ask for the content inline and re-paste it into each follow-up to revise it.
Generate it as an Artifact, which provides a persistent, editable canvas.
Save it to a Project so the text itself becomes directly editable.
Request a downloadable file and edit it outside Claude.
rationale: Artifacts provide a persistent, editable surface for content the associate revises over time. Inline replies are ephemeral, Projects store shared context rather than serving as an editing canvas, and exporting a file moves the work out of Claude rather than keeping it revisable in place.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: An associate is about to build an AI-assisted workflow. What should they do first?
options: Select a model and begin prompting so the team can learn by iterating.
Analyze the requirements and use case, including inputs, outputs, and success criteria.
Automate the full end-to-end process in the first release.
Standardize on the highest plan tier before scoping the work.
rationale: Solution design starts by understanding the requirements and use case, inputs, outputs, and success criteria, before choosing tools. Jumping to prompting, automating everything at once, or committing budget first all skip the foundational analysis.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Overview

stem: An associate wants to redesign a manual reporting process using Claude. What is the best early step?
options: Replace every human step with Claude before measuring the current process.
Automate only the steps that are already automated to minimize disruption.
Map the current process to identify high-value, lower-risk steps where Claude adds the most benefit.
Begin drafting prompts before documenting how the process works today.
rationale: Process optimization begins by mapping the current workflow and targeting high-value, lower-risk steps. Wholesale replacement ignores risk, automating already-automated steps adds no value, and drafting prompts before understanding the process is premature.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Overview

stem: When communicating the value of a Claude-based solution to stakeholders, which approach is most credible?
options: Emphasize only the benefits so stakeholders adopt the tool quickly.
Present realistic benefits together with limitations and where human oversight is required.
Guarantee the system will eliminate errors and reduce headcount.
Defer any discussion of limitations until after deployment.
rationale: Credible communication pairs realistic benefits with honest limitations and the points needing human review, setting correct expectations. Benefit-only messaging, error-free guarantees, and hiding limitations all erode trust and lead to overreliance.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Overview

stem: An associate is integrating Claude into an existing approval workflow. Which design is safest?
options: Full autonomy for Claude so approvals are never delayed by review.
A checkpoint that runs only after actions have already been executed.
A human review and approval checkpoint before consequential actions are finalized.
Claude reviewing its own outputs in place of human sign-off.
rationale: Responsible augmentation keeps a human review/approval checkpoint before consequential actions are finalized, preserving accountability. Full autonomy and self-review remove human judgment, and a post-hoc checkpoint reviews actions only after harm could already have occurred.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Overview

stem: A new use case has unclear requirements. How can Claude best support the design phase?
options: By committing to a single solution immediately to save time.
By helping analyze requirements, surface options, and draft a plan for the team to review.
By finalizing and deploying the design without team input.
By deferring all analysis until the requirements are fully fixed.
rationale: In early, ambiguous design, Claude is valuable for analyzing requirements, generating and comparing options, and drafting plans that humans evaluate and refine. Committing early, deploying without review, or waiting for perfect requirements all forgo that value.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Overview

stem: Which of the following are appropriate uses of Claude in workflow and solution design? (Select THREE.)
options: Drafting a project plan for human review
Researching options and summarizing trade-offs
Prototyping and iterating on a process design
Making a final hiring decision autonomously
Approving legal contracts without human review
rationale: Drafting plans for review, researching trade-offs, and prototyping designs are appropriate, human-in-the-loop uses. Autonomous final hiring decisions and approving contracts without review are consequential actions that exceed appropriate scope and require human judgment.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Overview

stem: An associate encounters a use case that requires custom API integration and agentic orchestration beyond their technical skill set. What is the correct action?
options: Build the integration independently despite lacking the technical background.
Recognize the scope boundary and escalate to a Claude Architect or Developer.
Conclude the use case cannot be delivered and close it out.
Substitute a manual workaround and leave it out of the design documentation.
rationale: Recognizing scope boundaries and escalating complex technical implementation to Architects/Developers is a defining associate competency. Building beyond one's skills risks a poor solution, declaring it impossible is premature, and an undocumented workaround hides risk and scope.
referenceText: Anthropic Docs — Overview

stem: An associate is redesigning a manual weekly-reporting workflow to incorporate Claude. Before piloting the change, they define a target metric and capture the current baseline. Why is defining a success measure up front most important?
options: It establishes a procurement budget for the tooling before the pilot begins.
It lets the associate objectively assess whether the redesign improved quality, speed, or cost against the baseline.
It guarantees the workflow will need no further changes after launch.
It benchmarks the team's throughput against departments running unrelated processes.
rationale: A pre-defined success measure with a baseline lets the associate objectively judge whether the redesign delivered the intended improvement. The other options describe unrelated goals (budgeting, apples-to-oranges comparison) or an unrealistic guarantee that no iteration will be needed.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) — Domain 4: Workflow Integration and Solution Design

stem: A compliance stakeholder asks whether Claude can 'just handle the entire' regulatory filing review end to end. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Confirm full automation is feasible so reviewers can be reassigned immediately.
Have Claude draft and submit filings, with spot-checks only when downstream errors are reported.
Explain which steps Claude can accelerate and where human validation and controls must remain.
Defer the question, since automation scope is decided outside the associate's team.
rationale: Setting accurate expectations in a regulated process means explaining Claude's real contribution while being clear about the human validation and controls that must stay in place. Promising full automation or minimal spot-checks removes essential oversight; deferring avoids the associate's responsibility to advise.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) — Domain 4: Workflow Integration and Solution Design

stem: Which approach best demonstrates using Claude to support iteration in solution design?
options: Generating a single comprehensive draft and treating it as final to save review cycles.
Having Claude produce a draft, gathering stakeholder feedback, and refining the design across several rounds.
Asking Claude for several unrelated drafts and selecting one without further changes.
Locking the first version early so the design stops shifting during the project.
rationale: Iteration means drafting, incorporating feedback, and refining across rounds — the loop Claude accelerates. The distractors describe one-and-done or premature-lock approaches that abandon iteration.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) — Domain 4: Workflow Integration and Solution Design

stem: An associate wants Claude to help build a realistic project timeline. Which framing will most improve the output?
options: Ask for the most aggressive timeline possible and adjust only if the team objects.
Request a generic timeline first and add scope and constraints only in later prompts.
Provide scope, milestones, dependencies, and known constraints so the plan is grounded in reality.
Give minimal detail so Claude can propose an unconstrained ideal schedule.
rationale: A realistic, reviewable plan needs real inputs — scope, milestones, dependencies, and constraints — supplied up front. The other framings either optimize for speed over feasibility or withhold the context needed to produce a grounded estimate.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) — Domain 4: Workflow Integration and Solution Design

stem: Claude is introduced to augment, not replace, a financial analyst's work. How does the analyst's role primarily shift?
options: The analyst approves Claude's outputs without independent checks to capture the time savings.
The analyst directs the work, validates outputs, and applies domain judgment Claude cannot.
The analyst transitions to formatting and delivering whatever Claude produces.
The analyst reviews only the outputs that downstream consumers flag as questionable.
rationale: Augmentation keeps the human directing the work, validating outputs, and applying judgment Claude lacks, while Claude handles drafting and analysis support. The distractors all describe abdicating review — rubber-stamping, passive formatting, or reactive-only checking.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) — Domain 4: Workflow Integration and Solution Design

stem: An associate is presenting a Claude-enabled workflow to leadership for approval. What should a well-balanced proposal include?
options: The projected benefits and a commitment that outputs will be error-free.
Expected benefits, key risks, required controls, and how outputs are validated.
The efficiency gains, with risks deferred to a review after the workflow is live.
A detailed cost model, leaving oversight and validation to the implementation phase.
rationale: A balanced proposal gives leadership the full picture: benefits, risks, controls, and the validation approach. The distractors omit risk/oversight up front or make an unrealistic error-free guarantee.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) — Domain 4: Workflow Integration and Solution Design

stem: An associate is triaging incoming requests. Which two signals indicate a task should be escalated beyond an associate's scope to an Architect or Developer? (Select TWO.)
options: The task requires building custom software integrations against production APIs.
The task requires designing an enterprise-scale, multi-agent orchestration system.
The task requires drafting a recurring status update from meeting notes.
The task requires summarizing a lengthy stakeholder interview transcript.
The task requires configuring a Project with custom instructions for the team.
rationale: Custom API integration development and enterprise-scale agentic system design require Architect/Developer expertise and belong outside the associate's scope. Drafting from notes, summarizing a transcript, and configuring a Project are all within associate scope.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) — Domain 4: Workflow Integration and Solution Design

stem: During a redesign, an associate realizes the proposed automation would eliminate a manual review step that currently catches costly billing errors. What is the right design decision?
options: Proceed with the automation, since the historical error rate has been low.
Preserve the control, or replace it with an equivalent automated check, before automating.
Remove the control now and add monitoring only if errors begin to surface.
Automate the step and shift accountability for any errors to the downstream team.
rationale: Responsible integration preserves controls that prevent costly errors, or substitutes an equivalent check, rather than trading safety for speed. Removing the control on the basis of past low rates, deferring monitoring, or shifting blame all leave the risk unmanaged.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) — Domain 4: Workflow Integration and Solution Design

stem: An associate uses Claude during requirements analysis for a new workflow. Which use is most appropriate?
options: Have Claude generate a complete requirements set from scratch to shorten discovery.
Have Claude structure and clarify the stated requirements and surface gaps for the team to confirm.
Have Claude finalize project scope so stakeholder sign-off can be skipped.
Have Claude decide which stakeholder constraints can be dropped to simplify delivery.
rationale: Claude helps organize and pressure-test stated requirements and flag gaps, which the team then confirms. Inventing requirements, finalizing scope without review, or unilaterally dropping constraints all remove the human judgment that requirements analysis depends on.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) — Domain 4: Workflow Integration and Solution Design

stem: Which outcome most clearly indicates that a Claude-augmented workflow is well designed?
options: It removes every human touchpoint from the process.
It measurably improves a target outcome such as quality, speed, or cost while retaining appropriate human oversight.
It routes every task to the most capable available model regardless of need.
It produces longer, more comprehensive outputs than the previous process.
rationale: A good design measurably improves the intended outcome while keeping appropriate oversight — not merely removing people, maximizing model size, or producing more text.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) — Domain 4: Workflow Integration and Solution Design

stem: An associate wants every conversation in a Project to follow the same tone and rules without re-entering them each time. Where should this be configured?
options: In a saved prompt the associate pastes at the start of each new chat.
In the Project's custom instructions, which apply across all conversations in the Project.
In a reference document linked from the Project's description.
In each conversation's title, which Claude reads before responding.
rationale: Project custom instructions apply consistently across every conversation in that Project, making them the right place for shared tone and rules. Re-pasting prompts is error-prone, a linked doc is not automatically applied, and conversation titles do not set behavior.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: Which of the following is the most effective set of Project custom instructions?
options: Act as a knowledgeable assistant and always give helpful, accurate, and thorough answers.
You assist the onboarding team. Use a warm, plain-language tone, cite the uploaded HR policy when relevant, and flag anything requiring HR sign-off.
Respond to onboarding questions using your best judgment and keep the team's preferences in mind.
You support HR. Be professional, avoid mistakes, and make sure every answer is fully compliant with all policies.
rationale: Effective instructions are specific about role, tone, which sources to rely on, and escalation behavior — as in the onboarding-team option. The others sound reasonable but stay vague about audience, sources, and when to escalate, so they produce inconsistent behavior.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: An associate adds a Google Drive connector so Claude can reference team documents. Which responsibility is most important when doing so?
options: Grant the connector access to the entire shared drive so no needed file is missed.
Ensure only appropriate, permitted data is exposed and that access aligns with organizational policy.
Confirm the connector caches documents locally so Claude can work offline.
Adjust existing Drive permissions so Claude can read uniformly across all folders.
rationale: Connecting a data source requires ensuring only appropriate, permitted data is exposed and that access respects organizational policy and existing permissions. Granting maximal access or loosening permissions expands risk, and offline caching is not the governing concern.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: After a policy change, the documents uploaded to a Project's knowledge are now outdated. What is the correct maintenance action?
options: Add a note in the instructions warning users the knowledge may be out of date.
Update or replace the knowledge source so Claude references the current policy.
Tell users to manually correct Claude whenever the old policy appears in an answer.
Create a new Project and leave the outdated one in place for reference.
rationale: Configurations must be maintained: when source material changes, the knowledge should be updated or replaced so Claude reflects current information. Warning notes or manual corrections leave the stale source in play, and spawning a duplicate Project does not fix the outdated knowledge.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: What is the primary purpose of adding knowledge sources to a Project?
options: To reduce the number of custom instructions the Project needs.
To ground Claude's responses in the team's own reference documents for relevance and consistency.
To permanently retrain the underlying model on the team's data.
To restrict which team members can view the Project's conversations.
rationale: Knowledge sources ground responses in the team's reference material, improving relevance and consistency. They do not replace instructions, do not retrain the model, and do not control member access — the last two are common misconceptions this item tests.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: Which practices support responsible configuration and knowledge management in Projects? (Select THREE.)
options: Writing clear, specific Project instructions.
Keeping knowledge sources current as the underlying material changes.
Uploading sensitive documents before confirming they are permitted under policy.
Documenting what each Project is for and who maintains it.
Connecting every available data source so nothing is ever missing.
rationale: Clear instructions, current knowledge, and documented purpose/ownership are sound practice. Uploading sensitive data before a policy check and connecting sources indiscriminately both create governance and exposure risk.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: An associate is configuring a Project for the sales team. Which instruction content is most useful to include?
options: A broad directive to be persuasive and close deals quickly.
The audience, preferred tone, authoritative sources to rely on, and topics to avoid or escalate.
A full catalog of every product the company has ever sold, for completeness.
The names of the sales team members who will use the Project.
rationale: Useful configuration specifies audience, tone, authoritative sources, and escalation/avoidance rules so outputs stay consistent and appropriate. A vague persuasion directive, an exhaustive product dump, or a user roster do not shape reliable behavior.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: A Project has a Gmail connector enabled. Before relying on it, the associate should verify that:
options: Every team member has access to all connected mailboxes for flexibility.
The scope of mailbox access matches the task and complies with privacy and organizational policy.
The connector is granted the broadest scope available to avoid future access requests.
Emails are exported into the Project's knowledge so access can be revoked afterward.
rationale: Enabling a connector requires confirming the access scope fits the task and complies with privacy and organizational policy — not maximizing access or copying data out. Broad access and unnecessary exports increase exposure rather than reduce it.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: Two associates jointly maintain the same Project. To prevent conflicting configurations, they should:
options: Split the Project into two copies so each can configure independently.
Agree on the Project's purpose and coordinate updates to instructions and knowledge.
Let whoever edited most recently override the other's changes by default.
Avoid editing instructions and rely on per-conversation prompts instead.
rationale: Shared configurations need a clear agreed purpose and a coordinated process for updating instructions and knowledge, which prevents conflicting behavior. Splitting into copies fragments the source of truth, last-writer-wins loses intent, and avoiding instructions defeats the point of a shared Project.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: Why should an associate periodically review a Project's instructions and knowledge sources?
options: Because instructions degrade in quality the longer they remain unchanged.
Because business needs, policies, and source documents evolve, and the configuration must stay accurate.
Because reviewing the Project resets the team's usage limits.
Because Claude requires periodic reconfiguration to retain access to the Project.
rationale: Ongoing review keeps the configuration aligned with evolving business needs, policies, and source documents so outputs stay accurate. Instructions do not decay on their own, reviews do not affect usage limits, and no periodic reconfiguration is needed to retain access.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: An associate reports that Claude in a shared Project keeps ignoring a required formatting rule despite it being requested. What is the most effective first step to diagnose the problem?
options: Start a new Project and migrate only the knowledge files across
Add the formatting rule again in every individual message as a workaround
Confirm the rule is stated clearly in the Project's custom instructions and is not contradicted by other guidance
Switch to a larger Claude model in the hope it follows formatting more reliably
rationale: When a Project behaves inconsistently, the custom instructions are the first place to check: verify the rule is present, unambiguous, and not overridden by conflicting guidance. Recreating the Project, repeating the rule per message, or switching models does not address a likely instruction defect.
referenceText: Claude Help Center - What are Projects

stem: An associate is assembling the knowledge base for a customer-support Project. Which source is most appropriate to add?
options: The current, approved support policies and product documentation
A broad archive of loosely related PDFs to maximize coverage
An early draft of the support policy that has not yet been approved
Historical chat transcripts that still contain customer personal data
rationale: Grounding a support Project in current, approved, relevant documentation produces accurate, on-policy responses. Loosely related material adds noise, unapproved drafts risk incorrect guidance, and transcripts with personal data introduce privacy exposure.
referenceText: Claude Help Center - What are Projects

stem: After the company launches a new product line, an associate maintaining a support Project should:
options: Add the new product's documentation and revise the instructions to cover it
Remove all existing product documentation and rebuild the Project from scratch
Leave the Project unchanged and rely on Claude's general knowledge of the product
Lock editing on the Project so the configuration cannot drift over time
rationale: Keeping a Project current means adding the new documentation and updating instructions so Claude can support the expanded scope. Wiping existing docs loses coverage, doing nothing leaves gaps, and locking the Project prevents necessary maintenance.
referenceText: Claude Help Center - What are Projects

stem: A project manager wants to upload a spreadsheet of customer names and account numbers to Claude for trend analysis, but company policy restricts sharing regulated personal data. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Upload the file unchanged because the analysis stays internal
Remove or anonymize the personal identifiers before uploading, consistent with policy
Upload the file but instruct Claude in the prompt not to store the data
Abandon the analysis because the data cannot be used at all
rationale: Applying data-sensitivity safeguards means anonymizing regulated identifiers so the analysis can proceed without exposing protected data. Internal use does not waive the policy, a prompt instruction is not a valid control over data handling, and abandoning the task is unnecessary once identifiers are removed.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: Which of the following is the clearest example of an inappropriate use of Claude in a business setting?
options: Drafting an internal newsletter for a colleague to review
Summarizing a publicly released quarterly report
Presenting unverified Claude output to clients as authoritative legal advice
Generating a list of themes for an upcoming team event
rationale: Passing off unverified AI output as authoritative professional legal advice creates real legal and client risk and is inappropriate. Drafting for review, summarizing public information, and brainstorming are routine, low-risk uses.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: An associate is asked to feed confidential employee health information into a general-purpose tool that has no policy clearance for such data. What is the right action?
options: Proceed, since completing the request quickly is the priority
Stop and follow the privacy and data-sensitivity policy, obtaining clearance or anonymizing before any use
Upload the data and delete the conversation immediately afterward
Ask a colleague outside the team to review the raw data first
rationale: Sensitive health data triggers privacy and regulatory obligations, so the associate must follow policy and obtain clearance or anonymize before any use. Speed does not override policy, deleting a conversation does not undo unauthorized processing, and sharing raw data more widely increases exposure.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: Which practice best reflects responsible AI use when Claude's output will inform a decision that affects individuals?
options: Automate the decision end-to-end to remove human bias from the process
Keep a human accountable for the decision and review outputs for accuracy and bias
Treat the output as neutral because the model has no personal stake in the outcome
Avoid recording that AI contributed so the decision looks fully human-made
rationale: Decisions affecting individuals require human accountability and active checks for accuracy and bias. Full automation removes oversight, assuming neutrality ignores model bias, and hiding AI involvement undermines transparency.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: Under typical organizational AI policy, which of the following are appropriate uses of Claude? (Select THREE.)
options: Drafting internal documentation for human review
Summarizing publicly available information
Producing final medical diagnoses to give directly to patients
Brainstorming marketing campaign ideas
Submitting regulated personal data without applying safeguards
rationale: Drafting for review, summarizing public information, and brainstorming are appropriate, low-risk uses. Producing final medical diagnoses substitutes AI for regulated professional judgment, and submitting regulated personal data without safeguards violates data-protection obligations.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: An associate encounters a task that conflicts with the organization's AI usage policy. What should they do?
options: Follow the policy and seek guidance or approval before proceeding
Set the policy aside so the deadline can be met
Treat the policy as advisory rather than binding
Complete the task and document the exception afterward
rationale: Organizational AI policies are binding, so a conflict should be resolved by following the policy and seeking approval before proceeding. Ignoring the policy, treating it as optional, or acting first and justifying later all bypass required controls.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: An organization is considering Claude to help screen job applicants. Which is the key ethical concern to address?
options: The model's average response latency during screening
The risk of biased or discriminatory outcomes affecting applicants
Whether outputs are formatted as prose or bullet points
The number of applications the system can process per hour
Whether the interface matches the company's branding
rationale: Using AI in consequential decisions like hiring raises the ethical risk of biased or discriminatory outcomes, which must be actively guarded against. Latency, formatting, throughput, and branding are operational concerns, not the core ethical issue.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: Before pasting a contract containing another company's confidential terms into Claude, an associate should:
options: Paste it freely, since confidentiality terms do not extend to AI tools
Confirm the use is permitted under the confidentiality obligations and organizational policy first
Redact only the counterparty's name and paste the remaining terms
Post the terms to a public forum first to check whether they are already public
rationale: Third-party confidential information carries contractual obligations, so the associate must confirm permitted use under the confidentiality terms and policy before uploading. Assuming AI is exempt, redacting only the name, or publicly disclosing the terms all breach or worsen the confidentiality risk.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: An associate realizes that a workflow they built might expose customers' regulated data. What is the most responsible immediate step?
options: Continue the workflow and address the exposure at the next review cycle
Pause the workflow and escalate the privacy risk for review and remediation
Delete the affected customer records to remove the exposure
Continue the workflow but stop logging its activity
rationale: A potential exposure of regulated data warrants pausing the workflow and escalating for review and remediation. Continuing prolongs the exposure, deleting records can destroy evidence and violate retention rules, and disabling logging conceals rather than resolves the issue.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: Which statement best captures appropriate transparency about AI use in professional work?
options: Be transparent where stakeholders reasonably need to know AI contributed, consistent with policy
Conceal AI involvement to avoid undermining confidence in the work
Present AI-generated output as the author's own professional expertise
Disclose AI use only if someone explicitly asks about it
rationale: Responsible use includes disclosing AI involvement where stakeholders reasonably need to know and policy requires it. Concealing involvement, misrepresenting output as personal expertise, and disclosing only when asked all fall short of appropriate transparency.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: An associate wants to use Claude to prepare tailored tax advice for external clients. What is the most appropriate stance?
options: Provide Claude's output to clients as definitive professional tax advice
Use Claude to support a qualified professional's judgment rather than to replace regulated advice
Deliver the output without professional review to speed up turnaround
Attach a disclaimer and otherwise treat the output as fully reliable
rationale: Regulated professional advice requires qualified human judgment, so Claude should support the professional rather than substitute for them. Presenting output as definitive advice, skipping review, or relying on a disclaimer while treating unverified output as reliable all misplace accountability.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: Which of the following are data-sensitivity red flags that should prompt extra caution before using Claude? (Select THREE.)
options: Personally identifiable information (PII)
Protected health or financial data
A company press release approved for public distribution
Another party's confidential information
A blog post already published on the public website
rationale: PII, protected health or financial data, and third-party confidential information all trigger heightened caution and safeguards. An approved press release and an already-published blog post are public and do not raise the same concern.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: What is the primary reason to align Claude usage with the organization's governance standards?
options: Manage legal, privacy, and reputational risk while enabling responsible value
Slow the pace of adoption so teams are forced to proceed cautiously
Restrict AI use to a small group of pre-approved staff
Create enough process overhead to discourage experimentation
rationale: Governance alignment exists to manage legal, privacy, and reputational risk while still enabling responsible, valuable use. It is not intended to slow teams down, gatekeep access, or add overhead for its own sake.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: An associate is unsure whether a particular use case for Claude is permitted. What is the best action?
options: Check the organization's AI policy and, if still unclear, ask the appropriate owner before proceeding
Assume it is permitted unless someone raises an objection
Proceed on a small scale to test whether it is allowed
Avoid the use case permanently to stay on the safe side
rationale: When permitted use is unclear, the associate should consult the AI policy and escalate to the appropriate owner for guidance. Assuming permission, quietly piloting to see if anyone objects, or abandoning potentially valuable work all skip the right resolution path.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: Which action is the best example of applying a privacy safeguard proactively?
options: Redact or pseudonymize identifiers before analysis so individuals cannot be re-identified
Upload raw customer records to save preparation time
Share the dataset broadly to gather more feedback on the analysis
Rely on deleting the conversation after the analysis is complete
rationale: Redacting or pseudonymizing identifiers before analysis is a proactive control that reduces the risk of identifying individuals. Uploading raw records, broadening access, and deleting afterward do not prevent the exposure at the point of use.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: A vendor asks an associate to share internal data classified 'restricted' by sending it through Claude. What is the correct response?
options: Decline or escalate, since restricted data must be handled according to its classification and policy
Share it because the vendor has a legitimate business need
Reclassify the data as public so that it can be shared
Send a redacted subset without seeking approval first
rationale: Data classified 'restricted' must be handled according to its classification, so the associate declines or escalates. A vendor request does not override classification, associates cannot unilaterally reclassify data, and sharing even a subset without approval bypasses the required control.
referenceText: CCAO-F Exam Guide (v1.0) - Domain 6: Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use

stem: A prompt consistently returns answers that miss the point of the request. What is the most effective first troubleshooting step?
options: Diagnose the prompt: clarify the goal, add missing context, and specify the desired output
Resend the identical prompt several times and average the responses
Switch to a different tool without changing the prompt
Lengthen the prompt by restating the same request in several different ways
rationale: Underperforming prompts are usually fixed by diagnosing and improving them - clarifying the goal, supplying context, and specifying the output format. Repeating the same prompt, changing tools, or padding with restatements does not address the underlying prompt weakness.
referenceText: Claude Docs - Prompt engineering overview

stem: An associate's marketing summaries keep coming back accurate but generic, reading the same regardless of who will receive them. What is the most effective way to make the outputs specific and relevant?
options: Regenerate the same prompt several times and keep whichever reply reads best.
Specify the audience, purpose, and concrete constraints so Claude can tailor the response.
Raise the maximum output length so the reply contains more detail.
Increase response randomness so answers vary more between runs.
rationale: Generic output improves when the prompt supplies the audience, purpose, and concrete constraints, giving Claude what it needs to tailor the response. Regenerating, lengthening, or adding randomness changes surface variation but does not add the missing context that causes genericness.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: An associate uses a multi-part prompt that asks for several sections, but Claude reliably omits one of them. What is the most dependable fix?
options: Explicitly enumerate every required section, or split the request into sequential steps.
Shorten the prompt so Claude has fewer instructions to track.
Combine all the parts into a single sentence so they stay together.
Resubmit the identical prompt until every section happens to appear.
rationale: When parts get dropped, decomposing the task or explicitly listing each required section makes coverage reliable. Shortening, compressing into one sentence, or resubmitting unchanged do not address why a section is being missed.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: A long working conversation has gradually drifted off-topic and the quality of Claude's responses is declining. What is the best way to restore focus?
options: Continue in the same thread and add more detail to steer it back on track.
Summarize the key context and continue in a new, focused conversation.
Switch to a more capable model to overcome the accumulated drift.
Clear everything and start over without carrying any prior context.
rationale: Drift in a long thread is best resolved by summarizing the essential context and restarting a focused conversation, preserving what matters while shedding the noise. Continuing the drifted thread, changing model tier, or starting with no context all fail to carry forward the needed context cleanly.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: An output underperforms against expectations. Which of the following are sound troubleshooting steps? (Select THREE.)
options: Clarify the instructions and the success criteria.
Resubmit the identical prompt and expect a different result.
Provide an example of the desired output.
Raise the randomness setting and accept whatever the next run produces.
Break the task into smaller, sequential steps.
