rationale: Clarifying instructions and success criteria, providing an example, and decomposing the task are all effective, evidence-based fixes. Resubmitting an unchanged prompt or simply increasing randomness does not diagnose or address the underlying cause.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: After several rounds of iteration, an associate lands on a prompt that produces excellent results for a recurring task. To optimize the broader team workflow, what should they do next?
options: Save it as a reusable, documented template the team can apply consistently.
Rewrite it from scratch each time so it stays fresh.
Keep the wording deliberately loose so it stays flexible.
Use it only for this instance and discard it afterward.
rationale: Optimizing a workflow means capturing what works as a reusable, documented template so the whole team benefits consistently. Discarding it, keeping it loose, or rebuilding it each time throws away the proven result.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: Claude returns a plausible-sounding but factually incorrect answer in a workflow that runs regularly. Beyond correcting this one answer, what is the best way to reduce the ongoing risk?
options: Correct this answer and treat the error as a one-off.
Build a verification step into the workflow so factual claims are checked before use.
Lengthen the responses so more supporting detail is included.
Lower the randomness setting to prevent future factual errors.
rationale: A recurring accuracy risk is best mitigated by building a verification step into the workflow so factual claims are checked before use. Treating it as a one-off, adding length, or lowering randomness does not systematically catch future errors.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Reduce hallucinations

stem: An associate notices that a shared Project produces inconsistent outputs depending on which team member is using it. What is the most likely optimization?
options: Tighten the project's custom instructions so the shared behavior is well specified.
Ask each user to word their prompts differently to add variety.
Remove the project instructions so each chat starts fresh.
Give each user a separate project with their own instructions.
rationale: Inconsistent Project behavior across users usually traces to loose or underspecified shared instructions; tightening the project's custom instructions makes outputs more consistent for everyone. Removing instructions or fragmenting into per-user projects increases variation rather than reducing it.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: Feedback shows a workflow is accurate but too slow and expensive for the volume it now handles. Which optimization best balances quality against cost and latency?
options: Route routine, high-volume steps to a faster, lower-cost model and reserve the most capable model for genuinely complex steps.
Use the most capable model for every step to guarantee the highest quality.
Add a manual review after each step to catch any errors.
Leave the setup unchanged since the accuracy is already acceptable.
rationale: Optimizing for high volume means matching routine steps to a faster, cheaper model while reserving the most capable model for genuinely complex steps. Using the top model everywhere keeps cost and latency high, added manual steps slow it further, and doing nothing ignores the stated problem.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Choosing a model

stem: When iteratively adjusting an approach based on results, what should an associate rely on to know whether a change actually helped?
options: Whichever change is quickest to make, regardless of its effect.
Observed output quality and feedback, changing one variable at a time.
Several settings changed together so a fix is reached faster.
A fixed approach, accepting the current results as they are.
rationale: Effective optimization is evidence-driven: rely on observed output quality and feedback and change one variable at a time so you can attribute improvement to a specific change. Changing many things at once obscures cause and effect, and speed or inaction is not a basis for improvement.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: A prompt intermittently ignores a formatting requirement. Before assuming a deeper model limitation, what is the most practical thing to check first?
options: Whether the formatting requirement is stated clearly and not contradicted elsewhere in the prompt or instructions.
Whether a more capable model would be more likely to follow the format.
Whether raising the randomness setting would improve adherence.
Whether moving the requirement to the very end of the prompt would help.
rationale: Intermittent format misses most often come from an unclear or internally contradictory instruction; confirming the requirement is stated clearly and not contradicted elsewhere is the practical first check. Switching models, raising randomness, or repositioning the requirement are premature before the instruction itself is verified.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: An associate wants Claude to answer from a specific role perspective and tone in every chat within a Project, without retyping it each time. Per Claude's Projects documentation, where does this belong?
options: Restated at the start of each individual chat.
In the project's custom instructions, which every chat in the project inherits.
In the project's name and description.
In a document uploaded to the project's knowledge.
rationale: Project custom instructions are the shared prompt every chat in the project inherits, so role and tone are set once there. The name and description are informational and do not steer Claude, uploaded knowledge is reference content rather than behavioral instruction, and per-chat restatement defeats the purpose.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: For a custom Skill to activate at the right moment, Claude's documentation stresses that the most important element is:
options: A clear, specific description that tells Claude when to use the skill.
The number of scripts and resources bundled with the skill.
The overall length and detail of the SKILL.md file.
Placing the skill near the top of the skills list.
rationale: Skills load through progressive disclosure based on their description, so a precise description of when to use the skill is what lets Claude select it correctly. Bundled scripts, file length, and ordering do not determine whether the skill fires.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: An associate has a general working preference — 'always ask clarifying questions before starting' — that should apply everywhere. Per the documentation, where does this belong?
options: In a task-specific skill that loads only when relevant.
In custom instructions, which apply broadly across all conversations.
In a single chat message at the start of each session.
In the knowledge base of one specific project.
rationale: Custom instructions apply broadly across all conversations, which fits a universal working preference. Skills are task-specific and load only when relevant, a single chat message applies to just that conversation, and project knowledge is scoped to one project.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: Per Claude's documentation, Claude generates an artifact rather than answering inline when the content is:
options: Any response longer than a single sentence.
Significant and self-contained, and likely to be edited, iterated on, or reused outside the chat.
Any response that contains computer code.
Only when the user issues an explicit command each time.
rationale: The documented criteria are content that is significant and self-contained (typically over ~15 lines) and likely to be edited, iterated on, or reused outside the conversation. Length alone, presence of code, or an explicit command are not the defining triggers; brief conversational answers stay inline.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: An associate has a Markdown document artifact and wants to change a single paragraph without regenerating the whole document. What is the documented way to do this?
options: Highlight the paragraph, select 'Edit with Claude,' and describe the change.
Ask in chat and rely on Claude to locate the correct paragraph on its own.
Regenerate the artifact and manually copy over the unchanged parts.
Download the file, edit it externally, and re-upload it.
rationale: For Markdown documents, artifacts support in-place editing: highlight the text, choose 'Edit with Claude,' and describe the change, so you don't have to explain which section you mean. The other options are slower workarounds that the in-place editing feature is designed to avoid.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: After several revisions to an artifact, an associate wants to compare or return to an earlier version. What does Claude's artifacts feature provide?
options: A version selector for switching between saved versions.
No history; each edit permanently overwrites the previous version.
A single undo that reverts the entire conversation.
Version history, but only on Team and Enterprise plans.
rationale: Artifacts include a version selector that lets you switch between different versions. Edits are not permanent overwrites, the mechanism is version-specific rather than a full-conversation undo, and it is not gated to Team or Enterprise plans.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: When choosing between an artifact and an inline reply, an associate should favor an artifact mainly when the content is:
options: A brief factual answer to a question asked in passing.
Something the reader will refer back to, edit, or reuse outside the conversation.
A short acknowledgement or confirmation.
Any content that took Claude more than one step to produce.
rationale: Artifacts suit substantial, self-contained deliverables the reader will keep, edit, or reuse. Brief answers, acknowledgements, or content judged only by how many steps it took are better handled inline.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: According to the documentation, which of the following are true of Claude's artifacts? (Select TWO.)
options: They open in a dedicated window beside the chat.
They are limited to plain text and cannot contain code.
Editing an artifact changes Claude's memory of the original content.
You can switch between versions using a version selector.
rationale: Artifacts render in a dedicated window beside the chat and support a version selector for switching between versions. They support many content types beyond plain text (documents, code, HTML, SVG, diagrams, and more), and editing an artifact does not alter Claude's memory of the original content.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: An associate builds an AI-powered artifact and shares it with the team. According to the documentation, how is usage billed when teammates use it?
options: All usage is billed to the artifact's creator.
Usage counts against each user's own Claude subscription limits.
Each user must supply their own API key to run it.
Usage is free and does not count against anyone's limits.
rationale: For AI-powered artifacts, each user authenticates with their own Claude account and usage counts against that user's own subscription limits, with no cost to the creator and no API keys required.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: A colleague on the free plan asks how many Projects they can create. Per Claude's documentation, the answer is:
options: Projects are a paid-only feature; free users get none.
Free users can create a limited number of projects, while paid plans allow more.
Every plan, including free, allows unlimited projects.
Each account is limited to exactly one project.
rationale: Projects are available to free accounts with a capped number, while paid plans raise the limit and add enhanced project knowledge via retrieval. The exact free-tier cap is subject to change, so the defensible answer states that free users get a limited number and paid plans get more, rather than claiming projects are paid-only or unlimited.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: A team lead wants a dedicated space where a set of reference documents is available to every related conversation and each new conversation automatically starts with the same standing instructions. Which Claude feature is designed for this?
options: A single long-running conversation that everyone keeps appending to
Custom instructions configured once at the account level
A Project, which pairs a shared knowledge base with instructions that every chat in it inherits
A Skill bundled together with the reference documents
rationale: Projects are self-contained workspaces that pair a knowledge base with project instructions; uploaded content is available across all chats and every chat inherits the instructions. A single conversation does not persist standing instructions across new chats, account-level custom instructions do not carry a shared document set, and a Skill packages a procedure, not a per-workspace knowledge base.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: On a paid plan, a project's knowledge base has grown to hundreds of documents. According to the documentation, how does the project make all of that content usable to Claude?
options: It retrieves the most relevant excerpts using Retrieval Augmented Generation, which scales project knowledge on paid plans
It loads every document in full into each message of the conversation
It keeps only the most recently uploaded document and ignores the rest
It summarizes the documents once and discards the originals
rationale: Enhanced project knowledge on paid plans (Pro, Max, Team, Enterprise) uses RAG to scale large knowledge bases, retrieving relevant content rather than loading everything. Documents are not loaded in full into every message, older files are not silently dropped, and originals are retained.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: An associate wants a quick, private one-off conversation that Claude will not save to chat history or use to build memory. Which option fits?
options: Start a new Project to hold the conversation
Use an incognito chat, started from the ghost icon on chats outside a project
Turn off custom instructions for that one chat
Finish the conversation and delete it immediately afterward
rationale: Incognito chat, launched from the ghost icon on chats outside a project, creates a temporary conversation that isn't saved to history or used for memory. Projects persist chats, toggling custom instructions does not affect saving, and deleting afterward still means the chat was saved during use.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use incognito chats

stem: Which statement about Claude's ability to search your past chats matches the documentation?
options: It is available on every plan, including the free plan
It is offered on paid plans, uses Retrieval Augmented Generation, and appears as a tool call in the conversation
It functions only within incognito chats
It requires you to export and re-upload your conversations first
rationale: Searching past chats is a paid-plan capability that uses RAG and surfaces as a tool call. It is not free-plan (memory from history is separate and broader), incognito chats are excluded from history, and no manual export/re-upload is required.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Chat search and memory

stem: An associate wants Claude to recall details from a project's conversations within that project, without that context surfacing in unrelated work. Per the documentation, project memory is:
options: Shared across every project and standalone chat in the account
Kept as a separate memory for each project, so contexts stay contained
Disabled entirely whenever a project is in use
Available only to organization administrators
rationale: Claude maintains a separate memory per project so contexts stay contained, and global memory synthesis excludes chats inside projects. It is neither globally shared, disabled in projects, nor admin-only.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Chat search and memory

stem: A stakeholder asks Claude to generate a video of a concept. Based on Claude's documented capabilities, the accurate response is that Claude:
options: Generates native video when prompted
Accepts text and image input and produces text output, with no native image, audio, or video generation
Generates video, but only on Enterprise plans
Generates audio narration but not video
rationale: Claude is a text-and-image-input, text-output model with no native image, audio, or video generation. The plan level does not unlock video generation, and audio generation is likewise not a native capability.
referenceText: Claude Platform Docs — Features overview

stem: According to the documentation, once an incognito chat is closed, it:
options: Is moved into your regular chat history
Cannot be reopened
Is added automatically to the project's memory
Is emailed to the account owner
rationale: Incognito chats are temporary and cannot be reopened once closed. They are not archived to history, not folded into project memory, and not emailed to anyone; on Team/Enterprise they may be retained briefly for safety and compliance exports but are not restored to the user.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use incognito chats

stem: An associate wants Claude to produce a downloadable document, spreadsheet, or slide deck as a file. Per the documentation, this depends on which capability being turned on?
options: Incognito chat
Code execution and file creation, which also underpins artifacts
A dedicated media add-on plan
The project description field
rationale: Artifacts and the Anthropic document-creation Skills (Excel, Word, PowerPoint, PDF) require Code execution and file creation to be enabled; without it, artifacts are not supported. Incognito chat, a media plan, and the description field are unrelated to file generation.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: Per the documentation, which two statements about Projects are accurate? (Select TWO.)
options: Each chat in a project inherits the project's instructions
The project's name and description are read by Claude as part of its prompt
Uploaded knowledge is available to every chat in the project
Projects are limited to Enterprise plans
rationale: Every chat inherits the project instructions, and knowledge-base content is available across all of the project's chats. The name and description are informational for humans and not seen by Claude, and Projects are available on all plans (free users get a limited number).
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: An associate hopes Claude will clear an inbox by sending replies automatically through the Gmail connector. Per the documentation, Claude can:
options: Send messages on your behalf once the connector is authorized
Search, read, and draft emails with context, but you must send them yourself
Only delete and archive messages, not compose them
Compose and schedule messages to send later automatically
rationale: The Gmail connector lets Claude search, read, and draft emails with context, but Claude cannot send on your behalf — drafts are sent manually, keeping a human in the loop. It does not auto-send, auto-schedule, or limit itself to deletion.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: A team produces the same recurring deliverable and wants Claude to follow the identical structure and standards every time, without re-explaining them. The documented mechanism is to:
options: Paste the full standard into every prompt
Package the workflow as a Skill that Claude applies automatically when relevant
Rely on each member to describe the format at the start of every chat
Store the standard in the project's name and description
rationale: Skills package proven workflows and standards so Claude applies them automatically when relevant, giving consistent output without re-explaining. Pasting into every prompt and per-chat re-explanation are exactly what Skills avoid, and the name/description fields are not read by Claude.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use skills in Claude

stem: An associate needs Claude both to reach a team's external system and to follow the team's exact procedure for using it. Per the documentation, the right combination is:
options: A Skill on its own
An MCP connector for access to the service, paired with a Skill for the procedure
Custom instructions on their own
An incognito chat with the procedure pasted in
rationale: MCP/connectors provide access to external services and data, while a Skill supplies the procedural how-to; the documentation notes they can be used together. A Skill alone does not grant system access, and custom instructions or a pasted-in incognito chat provide neither reliable access nor reusable procedure.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: A team wants Claude to prepare a meeting brief by drawing on calendar events, recent email, and Drive files. Per the documentation, this is enabled by:
options: Uploading screenshots of each application
Connecting the relevant Google Workspace connectors, which Claude queries only when you ask
Granting Claude standing background access to scan the apps continuously
Forwarding the items into an incognito chat
rationale: Google Workspace connectors (Calendar, Gmail, Drive) let Claude pull the needed data from the conversation, and Claude accesses it only when explicitly asked, retrieving the minimum required. It does not scan continuously in the background, and screenshots or incognito forwarding are not the documented approach.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: When setting up a Project, an associate places the key behavioral rules in the project's name and description. Per the documentation, why is this a mistake?
options: The name and description are the only fields Claude reads, so rules there override the instructions
Claude does not see the project's name or description; behavioral rules belong in the project instructions
Descriptions are capped at a few words and would truncate the rules
Project instructions are ignored once a knowledge base is attached
rationale: The name and description are informational for humans and not seen by Claude; behavioral guidance must go in the project instructions, which every chat inherits. Claude does read the instructions (not the reverse), and instructions remain in effect regardless of an attached knowledge base.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and manage projects

stem: In a Team or Enterprise organization, what must happen before individual members can authenticate Google Workspace connectors?
options: Each member emails Anthropic support to request access
An Owner or Primary Owner enables the connectors at the organization level
Members turn off memory before connecting
Nothing — the connectors are enabled for everyone by default
rationale: On Team and Enterprise plans an Owner or Primary Owner must enable Google Workspace connectors at the organization level before members can authenticate. Individual email requests, disabling memory, and default-on access are not the documented prerequisite.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: An associate worries that a newly configured connector could let Claude reach data the associate cannot normally see. Per the documentation, connectors:
options: Extend Claude's reach beyond the user's own permissions
Mirror your existing permissions, so Claude cannot reach anything you cannot already access
Bypass the source system's permissions entirely
Require you to share your account password in the chat
rationale: Connectors mirror the user's existing permissions — Claude cannot access anything the user cannot already access — and data is retrieved only when explicitly requested. They do not broaden access, bypass permissions, or require sharing a password.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: How are Anthropic's built-in Skills for creating Excel, Word, PowerPoint, and PDF files made available, per the documentation?
options: You must author each of them yourself before use
They are built in and invoked automatically when relevant, provided code execution is enabled
They are restricted to Enterprise plans
They must be triggered with a slash command on every use
rationale: Anthropic provides these document-creation Skills built in for all users; Claude invokes them automatically when relevant as long as code execution is enabled. Users need not author them, they are not Enterprise-only, and they do not require a manual command each time.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: Per the documentation, how do Skills avoid overloading the context window?
options: Every available skill's full contents load into each conversation
Through progressive disclosure — Claude loads only the relevant skill and only the details it needs
By clearing your chat history whenever a skill runs
By limiting each account to a single skill
rationale: Skills use progressive disclosure: Claude determines which skills are relevant and loads only the information it needs, avoiding context-window overload. It does not preload every skill in full, clear history, or cap accounts at one skill.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: An associate on a Pro plan uploads a custom Skill. By default, that skill is:
options: Shared automatically with the whole organization
Private to the associate's individual account
Published publicly for anyone to install
Removed automatically after a single use
rationale: Custom skills you upload are private to your individual account by default; sharing with colleagues or the organization is a Team/Enterprise capability. They are not auto-shared, published publicly, or deleted after use.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use skills in Claude

stem: A policy document referenced by a Project has changed. Per the documentation, the maintenance action that keeps the project's answers accurate is to:
options: Take no action, since project knowledge refreshes on its own
Update or replace the file in the project's knowledge base so chats reference the current version
Rename the project to signal the change
Remove the project's instructions
rationale: Project knowledge grounds Claude's responses, so updating or replacing the changed file keeps every chat referencing current information. Knowledge does not refresh automatically, and renaming the project or deleting instructions does not update the source material.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: A team lead is deciding whether to encode a recurring specialized workflow as a Skill or as a Project's custom instructions. Per the documentation, how do the two differ?
options: Custom instructions load only when a task matches; Skills apply to every conversation automatically
Custom instructions apply broadly across chats, while Skills are task-specific and load only when the work matches
Both apply broadly, but Skills silently override custom instructions whenever they conflict
Custom instructions are scoped per project, while Skills apply globally across the entire account
rationale: Custom instructions apply broadly across conversations, while Skills are task-specific, can hold far more detail, and activate only when the work matches the Skill's description. Option 0 reverses the two; option 2 invents a conflict-resolution rule the docs do not describe; option 3 misstates the scoping of both features.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: When building a custom Skill, what is the core file that defines it?
options: A configuration file named skill.json specifying triggers and permissions
A SKILL.md Markdown file containing the instructions, plus optional scripts and resources
A plain-text prompt saved to the project's knowledge base
A YAML manifest that points to an externally hosted instruction file
rationale: A Skill is a folder containing a SKILL.md Markdown file with instructions; simple Skills need no code, and executable scripts and resources can be attached for advanced use. The other options describe file formats and hosting arrangements the documentation does not use.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: An associate on an Enterprise plan wants to run a sensitive one-off review in an incognito chat and assumes it leaves no trace anywhere. Which statement is accurate per the documentation?
options: Incognito chats are saved to chat history but are excluded from memory
Incognito chats aren't saved to history or memory, but on Team/Enterprise they're retained for a limited period for safety and appear in compliance exports
Incognito chats bypass all retention, including Team and Enterprise compliance exports
Incognito chats are available inside projects, making them ideal for sensitive per-project work
rationale: Incognito chats aren't saved to chat history or memory and can't be reopened, but on Team/Enterprise plans they are retained for a limited time for safety and are included in organizational exports and the Compliance API — so they are not a guarantee of non-retention. They are only available outside projects, which makes options 2 and 3 wrong.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use incognito chats

stem: An associate asks whether data retrieved through the Google Workspace connectors is used to train Anthropic's models. Per the documentation, Anthropic:
options: Uses your Gmail, Drive, and Calendar connector data to help improve its models
Does not train its models on your Gmail, Drive, or Calendar connector data
Trains on Drive documents but not on Gmail or Calendar content
Trains on connector data unless you opt out in your settings
rationale: Anthropic does not train its models on Gmail, Drive, or Calendar connector data; retrieved data is stored with its associated chat, so deleting the chat deletes that data. The other options describe partial or opt-out training regimes the documentation explicitly rules out.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: An associate is about to publish an artifact so people outside the company can use a tool they built. What privacy caution does the documentation give?
options: Published artifacts remain private to your organization, so any content is safe to include
Avoid including confidential or sensitive information, because publishing or sharing makes the artifact accessible to others
Recipients must sign in with a company account before they can view a published artifact
Sensitive data is automatically removed from an artifact before it is published
rationale: Publishing or sharing an artifact makes it accessible to others (recipients may not even need an account), so confidential or sensitive information should be avoided; for AI-powered artifacts you should also consider whether shared storage is used. The distractors falsely assume privacy or automatic redaction that the docs do not promise.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: A team handling confidential work for multiple clients wants persistent context for each client with no cross-client leakage. Per the documentation, the safest structure is:
options: A single shared project with global memory enabled so context is available everywhere
A separate project per client, since project memory and knowledge are scoped and don't bleed into other projects or global memory
One project per client, but sharing a single knowledge base across them for efficiency
One incognito chat per client to prevent any retention of client context
rationale: Each project has its own scoped memory, knowledge base, and chat history that don't bleed into other projects or global memory — a documented containment guardrail suited to per-client confidentiality. A shared project or shared knowledge base (options 0 and 2) allows leakage, and incognito chats (option 3) discard the very context the team wants to persist.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Chat search and memory

stem: When does Claude access your Gmail, Drive, or Calendar through a connector, according to the documentation?
options: Continuously in the background to keep an up-to-date index of your data
Only when you explicitly ask a question or request an action that requires it, retrieving the minimum information needed
On a scheduled interval so responses always reflect the latest content
Once at connection time, caching a full copy of your data for later use
rationale: Claude accesses connector data only when you explicitly ask something that requires it, and retrieves the minimum information needed — consistent with least-privilege handling. The other options describe background, scheduled, or bulk-copy access models the documentation rules out.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: An Enterprise admin needs to remove Google Workspace connector access for the entire organization. Per the documentation, they can:
options: Not revoke it, because connector access becomes permanent once enabled
Disable the connectors in Organization settings, where Owners and Primary Owners control org-level connector access
Only disable connectors per individual chat, since there is no organization-wide control
Contact support to make the change, because org-level connector settings are not self-service
rationale: On Team and Enterprise plans, Owners and Primary Owners can disable Google Workspace connectors for the whole organization in Organization settings, while individual users can also disable specific tools from the chat interface. The distractors deny org-level control that the documentation grants.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: An associate enabled a custom Skill, but Claude never seems to use it during relevant tasks. Per the documentation, the most likely cause is:
options: The SKILL.md file is too short to be recognized as a valid Skill
The Skill's description is too vague, so Claude doesn't recognize when to load it
The Skill was not attached to a project, so it never loads at all
Code execution is disabled, which prevents any Skill from activating
rationale: Skills load based on their description through progressive disclosure, so a vague description is the common reason a Skill fails to fire; tightening it to clearly state when to use the Skill resolves the issue. The other options invent length, attachment, or capability prerequisites that are not the documented cause.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: Claude is no longer producing artifacts or creating files for an associate. According to the documentation, the first thing to check is:
options: Whether the conversation has exceeded the model's context window
Whether Code execution and file creation is enabled in settings or capabilities
Whether the associate is on a paid plan rather than the free tier
Whether memory is enabled for the current project
rationale: Artifacts require Code execution and file creation to be enabled (Settings > Capabilities, or Organization settings on Team/Enterprise); without it, artifacts are not supported. Context length, plan tier, and memory settings are not the documented prerequisite for artifact creation.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: A Google connector stops working and returns a 'you may need to reconnect' error. Per the documentation, the remedy is to:
options: Delete and recreate the Claude account to reset the authorization token
Go to Customize > Connectors, disconnect the Google connector, then re-authenticate the next time you use it
Clear the browser cache and restart the app to refresh the session
Switch the connector to read-only mode in your Google account settings
rationale: The documented fix is to disconnect the Google connector under Customize > Connectors and re-authenticate the next time a Workspace feature is used; if problems persist, remove the Claude connection from Google account settings. Account deletion, cache clearing, and a read-only toggle are not the documented steps.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: An associate's long single chat keeps losing earlier context and mixing topics. A documented, structural way to keep specialized guidance available without overloading the context window is to:
options: Paste the full guidance at the top of every message in the chat
Encode the recurring guidance as a Skill, which loads only when relevant through progressive disclosure
Store the guidance in global memory so it is always present in context
Increase the model's context window in the account settings
rationale: Skills load on demand through progressive disclosure, keeping specialized instructions available without cluttering the context window — cleaner than pasting everything into every prompt. Global memory would keep the guidance always loaded (adding to the clutter), and the context window is not a user-adjustable setting.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: Anthropic's prompt-engineering documentation offers a 'golden rule of clear prompting.' What is it?
options: Keep every prompt as short as possible to avoid overwhelming the model
Show your prompt to a colleague with minimal context; if they're confused, Claude likely will be too
Always include at least five worked examples so the pattern is unambiguous
Test the prompt across several models before you rely on its output
rationale: The golden rule is to show your prompt to a colleague with minimal context and ask them to follow it; if they're confused, Claude probably will be too. The other options are reasonable-sounding practices but are not the stated golden rule.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: When writing prompts, the documentation suggests you think of Claude as which of the following?
options: A mind reader who can infer your unstated intent from a few keywords
A brilliant but brand-new employee with amnesia who needs explicit instructions and context
A search engine that responds best to short keyword queries
A domain expert who already knows your team's conventions and norms
rationale: Anthropic frames Claude as a brilliant but brand-new employee with amnesia: it lacks your norms and context, so the more precisely you explain what you want, the better the result. The distractors each assume prior context or keyword behavior the framing explicitly rejects.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: To convey a desired style or output format, the prompt-engineering guidance says the most effective approach is often to:
options: Stack descriptive adjectives such as 'clear,' 'professional,' and 'concise'
Provide one good example of the desired output
Describe the format in as much detail as possible without showing an example
Let Claude infer the format from the surrounding conversation
rationale: The docs note that one good example beats multiple adjectives describing what you want; showing input-output samples reliably steers format and style. Adjective-stacking, verbose description, and relying on inference are all less effective than a concrete example.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: Which instruction best reflects the documentation's advice to 'be specific'?
options: 'Keep it concise.'
'Write 5 bullets, each under 15 words.'
'Give me a professional summary.'
'Format this nicely for an executive audience.'
rationale: The guidance favors explicit, measurable constraints ('5 bullets, each under 15 words') over vague directives, which leave room for misinterpretation. The other three are all subjective and unmeasurable.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: Per the documentation, assigning Claude a role or expert persona via a system prompt primarily helps by:
options: Guaranteeing that the resulting output is factually accurate
Setting the appropriate perspective, tone, and vocabulary for the task
Removing the need to review or verify the output
Reducing the number of tokens the model must process
rationale: Role prompting defines an expert persona so Claude adopts the right perspective, tone, and vocabulary; it shapes communication style rather than guaranteeing accuracy, removing review, or saving tokens.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: In the documentation, the 'prompt chaining' technique means:
options: Sending the same prompt several times and comparing the outputs
Breaking a complex task into a sequence of smaller, connected prompts
Combining several unrelated requests into a single long prompt
Linking outputs from multiple different models into one response
rationale: Prompt chaining breaks a complex task into ordered steps (for example, outline first, then expand each section), improving reliability and allowing intermediate checks. The distractors describe repetition, bundling, and cross-model linking, none of which is prompt chaining.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: To reduce hallucinations, Anthropic's prompting guidance recommends explicitly:
options: Instructing Claude to always answer confidently and avoid hedging
Giving Claude permission to say 'I don't know' when it is unsure
Asking Claude to cite as many sources as possible for every claim
Requesting the most detailed answer Claude can produce
rationale: Explicitly allowing Claude to say 'I don't know' and flag uncertainty reduces fabricated answers, which matters most for factual and RAG-style tasks. Demanding confidence, maximal citations, or maximal length does not address hallucination and can worsen it.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: According to the documentation, which statement about prompt length is accurate?
options: Longer, more detailed prompts consistently produce better results
Longer prompts aren't always better; clarity and specificity matter more than length
Prompts should omit context so the model stays focused on the task
Short, keyword-style prompts outperform full-sentence instructions
rationale: The documentation warns against over-engineering: length is not inherently better, and verbosity without added structure can make outputs worse — clarity and specificity matter more. The other options overstate length, context omission, or keyword brevity.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: A team member wraps a source document in <document> tags and places the task instructions outside them. Per the prompt engineering documentation, the primary reason to use XML tags or clear delimiters is to:
options: Reduce the number of tokens the prompt consumes
Clearly separate instructions from the data and context so Claude can reliably tell them apart
Force Claude to respond using XML-formatted output
Prevent the prompt from being logged or retained
rationale: Tags/delimiters exist to separate instructions, context, and examples so Claude reliably distinguishes them. They do not primarily save tokens, do not dictate the response format, and have nothing to do with logging/retention.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering: use XML tags

stem: An associate refined a prompt on the current model and worries it will stop working when a newer model version is released. According to the documentation, prompt engineering:
options: Requires a full rewrite of every prompt with each model release
Generally carries over across model upgrades, an advantage over approaches like fine-tuning
Is locked to a single model version and cannot be reused
Applies only to fine-tuned models, not base models
rationale: Prompts work atop existing models and largely persist through upgrades, unlike fine-tuning which is tied to a version. They do not require rewriting each release, are not locked to one version, and apply to base models.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: A new user needs to build a prompt for a task but has no draft to start from. The documentation's recommended first step is to:
options: Write the longest possible prompt to cover every conceivable edge case
Use the prompt generator in the Claude Console to produce a first draft, then iterate
Wait for Anthropic to publish an official template for the task
Adapt an unrelated existing prompt and hope it transfers
rationale: The docs recommend the Console prompt generator for a solid first draft when starting from scratch, then iterating. Length alone is not the goal, no per-task templates are promised, and reusing an unrelated prompt is not the documented starting point.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: Which of the following are documented prompt-engineering techniques for improving Claude's responses? (Select THREE.)
options: Be clear and direct, stating explicitly what you want Claude to do
Provide examples of the desired input and output (multishot prompting)
Assign Claude a relevant role through a system prompt
Raise the temperature to its maximum to improve factual accuracy
Repeat each instruction several times to force compliance
rationale: Clear/direct instructions, examples (multishot), and role prompting via a system prompt are all documented techniques. Maximizing temperature harms factual reliability, and repeating instructions is not a documented technique.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: According to the 'be clear, direct, and detailed' guidance, giving Claude more relevant context about a task usually:
options: Improves its performance, much as more context helps a person complete a task
Slows the model down without changing output quality
Confuses the model and lowers the quality of the response
Has no measurable effect on the response
rationale: The docs state Claude, like a person, performs better with relevant background (purpose, audience, constraints). More context does not inherently slow it, confuse it, or leave quality unchanged.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Be clear, direct, and detailed

stem: An associate needs Claude to complete a single, one-off analysis they are unlikely to repeat. Per the documentation, the appropriate approach is to:
options: Build a reusable Skill before starting the task
Write a clear prompt with the needed context and refine it as you go
Set up a dedicated Project specifically for the one-off task
Wait until the workflow becomes repeatable before prompting at all
rationale: Good prompting suffices for one-off or exploratory work; Skills are for approaches you want applied consistently across many conversations. A Project or Skill is unnecessary overhead here, and delaying is not advised.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are Skills

stem: A team uses Claude's Research feature to support a due-diligence review. Per the documentation, an advantage for validating the results is that Research delivers:
options: Reports with easy-to-check citations so findings can be traced back to their sources
Concise summaries with sources omitted to keep them short
Conclusions drawn solely from Claude's internal knowledge
A ranked list of links without any synthesis
rationale: Research produces thorough answers with easy-to-check citations so claims can be verified. It does not omit sources, does not rely solely on internal knowledge, and synthesizes rather than returning bare links.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: Claude uses file creation to generate an Excel financial model. Before an associate relies on it for a decision, the documented best practice is to:
options: Trust the output because Claude generated the formulas
Review the formulas, data, and any sensitive content, treating the draft as a strong starting point
Discard it and rebuild the model manually to be safe
Share it with stakeholders and correct any issues if they surface
rationale: The guidance keeps a human in the loop: verify formulas, data, and sensitive content, treating the file as a strong first draft. Blind trust and share-then-fix skip verification; a full manual rebuild discards the value of the draft.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: An associate needs a polished, downloadable report file rather than an on-screen response. The documented capability that produces this is:
options: Code execution and file creation, which generates real .docx, .pdf, .xlsx, and .pptx files
Artifacts, which display a preview but cannot be exported as Office files
Extended thinking, which lengthens the on-screen answer
Projects, which store the conversation and files for later reference
rationale: Code execution and file creation outputs actual downloadable Office/PDF files with formatting, formulas, and charts. Artifacts render previews, extended thinking affects reasoning depth, and Projects organize context rather than produce deliverables.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: Per the 'when to use' guidance, extended thinking is best suited to:
options: Simple factual lookups that need a fast response
Complex reasoning tasks where Claude benefits from planning its approach before answering
Shortening every response Claude produces
Preventing Claude from calling any tools
rationale: Extended thinking suits complex reasoning, letting Claude plan before answering. It is unnecessary for simple lookups, does not shorten answers, and does not disable tool use.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Web search, extended thinking, and research

stem: According to the documentation, the final output of the Research feature is:
options: A longer, synthesized report with citations drawn from multiple sources
A single-sentence answer optimized for speed
A raw list of the sources consulted, without analysis
A spoken audio summary of the findings
rationale: Research synthesizes multiple web and internal sources into a longer, cited report. It is not a one-line answer, not a bare source list, and not an audio file.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Web search, extended thinking, and research

stem: An associate wants Claude to turn a messy CSV into a cleaned analysis with charts and a written summary. The most fitting documented approach is to:
options: Ask Claude how to perform the steps in Excel manually
Use code execution / file creation to clean, analyze, and visualize the data into a formatted output
Paste the CSV and request only a quick text summary
Rename the file so Claude recognizes the columns
rationale: Code execution lets Claude clean, analyze, and visualize data with Python and return formatted deliverables. Manual Excel guidance, a plain text summary, or renaming the file do not produce the analyzed, charted output requested.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: An associate wants to change a few sentences in a Markdown artifact without regenerating the whole document. The documented way to do this is to:
options: Highlight the specific text and choose 'Edit with Claude' to revise it in place
Regenerate the entire artifact from a revised prompt
Download the file, edit it externally, and re-upload it
Copy the text into a new conversation to make the change
rationale: Markdown artifacts support targeted in-place editing: highlight text and select 'Edit with Claude.' Full regeneration, external editing, or a new conversation are not the documented in-place method.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts

stem: An associate is deciding whether to enable web search for a question. Per the documentation, web search is the right choice when the request:
options: Depends on current, changing, or recent information beyond the model's training data
Concerns a stable, well-established historical fact
Is a creative writing exercise with no factual basis
Can be answered entirely from the model's own knowledge
rationale: Web search is for current, changing, or recent information outside the training data. Stable historical facts, creative writing, and questions answerable from internal knowledge do not require it.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Web search, extended thinking, and research

stem: Which outputs most warrant human verification before external use, consistent with the file-creation guidance? (Select TWO.)
options: The formulas and figures in a Claude-generated Excel model
A financial report being sent to a client
An internal brainstorming list for the associate's own reference
The file name chosen for a working draft
rationale: The guidance stresses reviewing formulas, data, and sensitive content, especially for high-stakes, client-facing deliverables. An internal brainstorming list and a file name are low-stakes and not the verification focus.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: The documentation notes that combining extended thinking with Research is useful because Claude can:
options: Plan its approach carefully and then carry out comprehensive, cited information gathering
Skip source gathering and answer from memory more quickly
Compress the final report down to a single line
Avoid using web search while still producing citations
rationale: Pairing the two lets Claude plan thoughtfully and then gather multi-source, cited information comprehensively. It does not skip sources, shorten the report to one line, or produce citations without searching.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Web search, extended thinking, and research

stem: An associate asks Claude to convert a Word report into a slide deck. Per the documentation, file creation:
options: Can convert content from one format to another while preserving or enhancing it
Cannot change a document's format and can only edit it in place
Supports spreadsheets only, not presentations
Requires the associate to retype the content into the new format
rationale: File creation supports cross-format conversion (e.g., Word to PowerPoint) while preserving or enhancing content. It is not format-locked, is not limited to spreadsheets, and does not require manual retyping.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: Which statement best reflects the documented purpose of citations in Research outputs?
options: They let you check the source behind each claim so you can trust the findings
They lengthen the report to make it appear more thorough
They are formatting decoration with no functional role
They remove the need to read the underlying report
rationale: Citations make findings verifiable by linking each claim to its source, supporting responsible evaluation. They are not padding, not decoration, and do not replace reviewing the report.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: For a quick, single factual lookup in the middle of a task, the documentation indicates the best-fit tool is usually:
options: A standard web search that returns an answer from one or two lookups
The full Research feature, which runs many sequential searches
Extended thinking with no tools enabled
Creating a new Project to store the question
rationale: A standard web search fits quick single lookups. Research is reserved for comprehensive, multi-source investigations; extended thinking adds reasoning depth, not fresh facts; a Project is for organizing context.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Web search, extended thinking, and research

stem: An associate is unsure whether Claude's output fully addresses a multi-part request. A sound documented habit is to:
options: Check the output against each part of the request and every required section
Assume it is complete if the formatting looks polished
Count the paragraphs to confirm the response is long enough
Share it now and address any gaps in a follow-up
rationale: Completeness is verified by mapping the output back to each requested part and required section. Polish, length, and share-then-fix are not evidence that all elements are present.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering overview

stem: An associate asks Claude to turn a single CSV into a PowerPoint deck, a PDF, and an Excel workbook in one request. Per the file-creation documentation, what should the associate expect of each generated file?
options: Each file opens read-only and can only be edited from within Claude
The exported files strip formatting to guarantee cross-platform compatibility
Each file preserves professional formatting and opens directly in Microsoft Office or Google Workspace
Only the Excel workbook stays editable; the PDF and PowerPoint are flattened to images
rationale: The documentation states coordinated multi-format outputs retain professional formatting and open directly in Office or Google Workspace while remaining fully editable. Read-only/Claude-only editing and formatting-stripping contradict this; the workbook-only distractor invents a limitation the docs do not describe.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: A financial analyst receives a Claude-generated first draft of a client-facing report. Following the documented guidance, how should the analyst treat this draft?
options: As a finished deliverable that can be forwarded to the client without further review
As a strong starting point to verify and refine — checking data, formulas, and sensitive content — before sharing externally
As a rough outline with no usable value until it is fully rewritten by hand
As an artifact that becomes locked and uneditable once Claude generates it
rationale: The guidance frames generated files as a strong starting point that the associate still validates and refines before external use. Treating it as final skips required review; 'no value/full rewrite' and 'locked/uneditable' both misstate the documented workflow.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: An administrator is evaluating which subscription tiers can use Claude's code execution and file creation (Excel, Word, PowerPoint, PDF). According to the documentation, this capability is available on:
options: Enterprise and Team plans only
Pro and Max plans only
All plans — Free, Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise
Any paid plan, but not the Free plan
rationale: Code execution and file creation is available to all Claude users across Free, Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise on web, Desktop, and Mobile; paid tiers mainly add higher usage limits. Each distractor incorrectly excludes tiers the docs include.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: Per the documentation, what is the maximum file size for uploads and downloads with Claude's file features?
options: 10MB per file
30MB per file
100MB per file
500MB per file
rationale: The documented maximum is 30MB per file for both uploads and downloads; PDFs larger than 30MB can still be processed through the computing environment without loading into the context window. The other sizes are plausible-looking but incorrect.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: An associate enables Research, but Claude never begins investigating and no blue indicator appears. Per the documentation, what does Research require to function?
options: Extended thinking must be disabled first
Web search must be turned on
The account must be on an Enterprise plan
The conversation must be started in incognito mode
rationale: Research depends on web search being enabled; it is activated from the '+' menu and shows a blue indicator while running. Disabling extended thinking, an Enterprise requirement, and incognito are not prerequisites and are fabricated.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: Based on the 'when to use' guidance, Research is the best fit for a task that:
options: Answers a single well-defined factual question as quickly as possible
Requires comprehensive investigation across many sources (roughly five or more tool calls), synthesized into an in-depth, cited report
Must deliberately avoid citing any external sources
Involves lightweight lookups that never touch the web
rationale: Research suits comprehensive gathering that requires about five or more tool calls, synthesizing web and integration sources into a cited report. Quick single facts, avoiding citations, and no-web lookups are the scenarios the docs steer away from Research.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Web search, extended thinking, and research

stem: According to the documentation, how often does a plan's session-based usage limit reset?
options: Every 5 hours
Every 24 hours
At the start of each calendar month
Only when the user manually clears their chat history
rationale: Session-based usage limits reset every five hours; weekly limits are separate and reset at a fixed time each week assigned to the account. The other intervals misstate the documented reset cadence.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What is the Pro plan

stem: The documentation distinguishes usage limits from length limits. Which statement describes the distinction correctly?
options: Usage limits cap a single conversation, while length limits cap total monthly spend
Usage limits govern how much you use Claude over time across all conversations, while length limits govern how long or complex a single conversation can get
Both terms refer to the number of messages allowed within one five-hour session
Length limits determine billing, while usage limits determine model quality
rationale: Usage limits govern quantity over time across all conversations; length limits govern how long or complex any single conversation can become (hit it and you start a new chat or use projects). The distractors swap or conflate these definitions with billing and model quality.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: Per the Pro plan documentation, compared with the free service the Pro plan provides:
options: Identical usage to the free service, with only faster responses
At least five times the free service's per-session usage, plus priority access during high traffic and early access to new features
Roughly double the free usage, but without priority access
Direct API access in place of the chat interface
rationale: Pro offers at least five times the per-session usage of the free service, priority access during high-traffic periods, and early feature access (US$20/month). 'Identical', 'double', and 'API access instead of chat' all misstate the documented benefits.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What is the Pro plan

stem: A growing organization is sizing a Team plan. Per the documentation, how many seats does the Team plan support?
options: Up to 50 seats
Up to 150 seats, with organizations needing more moving to Enterprise
Up to 500 seats
An unlimited number of seats
rationale: Team plans support up to 150 seats; organizations that exceed this upgrade to Enterprise. The other seat counts are plausible but do not match the documented cap.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What is the Team plan

stem: An IT lead is comparing Team with Pro for a 40-person department. Which description of what the Team plan adds is accurate per the documentation?
options: It provides Pro's features with no additional organization-level controls
It adds centralized admin and billing, SSO and role-based access management, and workplace connectors, with usage limits applied per member
It pools a single shared usage limit across all team members
It bundles API usage credits into every seat
rationale: Team builds on Pro with centralized admin/billing, advanced identity and access management (SSO, JIT provisioning, role-based permissioning), and connectors — and usage limits are per member, not pooled. 'No added controls', 'pooled limits', and 'bundled API credits' each contradict the docs.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What is the Team plan

stem: An associate wants to keep output quality high while controlling token costs across many routine tasks. Per the usage-credits guidance, the recommended approach is to:
options: Use the most capable Opus model on every task to reduce the chance of retries
Use an efficient model (Haiku or the most recent Sonnet) for most tasks, reserving Opus for the genuinely hardest work
Disable extended thinking and web search on all conversations
Distribute tasks across several separate accounts
rationale: The guidance recommends choosing efficient models — Haiku or the latest Sonnet — for most tasks at lower token cost, keeping Opus for genuinely complex work. Defaulting to Opus everywhere raises cost; disabling features and splitting accounts are not the documented cost-control advice.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Manage usage credits

stem: An associate repeatedly hits a project's context limit while working with a large knowledge base. Per the documentation, which action best maximizes the available context?
options: Upload additional reference files so Claude has more material to draw on
Lean on the project's retrieval (RAG), keep project instructions concise, and remove unused files
Enable extended thinking on every message in the project
Paste the full knowledge base into each individual prompt
rationale: The docs recommend maximizing context by relying on projects' RAG, keeping instructions concise, removing unused files, and disabling extended thinking when not needed. Adding more files, forcing extended thinking, and pasting the whole knowledge base each consume more context, not less.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: Per the documentation, projects handle large amounts of information efficiently because they:
options: Automatically switch the conversation to a larger-context model
Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to load only the relevant content into the context window
Grant unlimited context on every plan
Summarize the entire knowledge base before every reply
rationale: Projects use RAG to work with large knowledge bases by loading only relevant content into context rather than everything at once. There is no automatic model swap, no unlimited context, and no full-base summarization per reply.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: Which statement about the availability of Claude's memory features matches the documentation?
options: Both memory from chat history and searching past chats are Enterprise-only
Memory from chat history is available on all plans, while searching past chats is limited to paid plans
Memory features are available only within incognito conversations
Memory from chat history is paid-only, while searching past chats is free on all plans
rationale: Memory from chat history is available to all plans (Free through Enterprise), while the separate 'search past chats' capability is limited to paid plans and uses RAG. The Enterprise-only, incognito-only, and reversed-availability options all misstate this split.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Chat search and memory

stem: Per the documentation, incognito chat is available to:
options: All Claude users
Paid plans only
Enterprise administrators only
Only users who have disabled memory
rationale: Incognito chat is available to all Claude users; it creates a temporary conversation, outside projects, that is not saved to history or memory. The other options invent tier or configuration restrictions.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use incognito chats

stem: An associate needs deeper, more careful reasoning on a hard analytical task and is deciding which feature to toggle. Per the documentation, the appropriate feature is:
options: Incognito chat, which isolates the reasoning steps
Extended thinking, which can be turned off when not needed to help conserve usage
Web search, which supplies the reasoning process
Renaming the project to signal that the task is complex
rationale: Extended thinking provides enhanced reasoning for complex tasks and can be turned off when not needed to optimize usage. Incognito, web search, and renaming a project do not provide the reasoning capability described.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: Per the usage-credits guidance, enabling usage credits lets paid subscribers (Pro, Max):
options: Receive unlimited usage with no further charges
Continue working past their included session limits, billed at standard API rates
Permanently remove the five-hour session reset
Automatically downgrade to a cheaper model once limits are reached
rationale: With usage credits enabled, paid subscribers can keep working after hitting session limits, billed at standard API rates, while the five-hour reset still applies. Usage is not unlimited/free, the reset is not removed, and there is no automatic model downgrade.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Manage usage credits

stem: After Claude creates a file in a conversation, the documentation says you can:
options: Only view it inline within the chat
Download it or save it directly to Google Drive
Only print it from the chat window
Access it solely through the API
rationale: Files Claude creates can be downloaded or saved directly to Google Drive from the conversation, ready to open in Office or Google Workspace. View-only, print-only, and API-only access each understate the documented options.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: Which two statements are accurate per the file-creation documentation? (Select TWO.)
options: Claude runs code in a private, sandboxed computing environment
The maximum file size is 30MB per file
File creation is restricted to Enterprise plans
Claude can generate native video files
rationale: File creation runs code in a private, sandboxed environment and supports up to 30MB per file. It is available on all plans (not Enterprise-only), and Claude does not generate native video files.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: An analyst must build a competitor comparison that consolidates pricing, feature sets, and market reputation across six companies into one sourced write-up. Which Claude capability is the documented best fit?
options: Research, which runs multiple chained searches and synthesizes a single cited report
A single web search query, which returns one set of results for the analyst to read
Extended thinking, which reasons at length before answering from what the model already knows
A Project, which stores the finished comparison as shared knowledge for future chats
rationale: Research is purpose-built for multi-source investigations like competitor comparisons: it runs several searches that build on each other and produces a synthesized, cited report. A single web search returns only one result set; extended thinking improves reasoning but does not gather sources; a Project stores context but does not perform the investigation.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: A consultant asks what sources Research can draw on when the relevant connectors are authorized. Per the documentation, Research can investigate across:
options: Only publicly available web pages retrieved in real time
Only files uploaded directly into the current conversation
The web plus Claude's training data, but no connected apps
The web plus connected internal context such as Gmail, Calendar, and Google Docs
rationale: Research cross-references both the open web and your connected internal context (Gmail, Calendar, Google Docs) so it can reconcile internal and external information. The other options each omit either the web or the connected internal sources.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: An associate wants one conversation that ingests a CSV, builds a financial model, drafts a memo, and produces a slide deck. According to the file-creation documentation, Claude:
options: Produces one output format per conversation, so each deliverable needs a separate thread
Can take a single data source and produce coordinated multi-format outputs in one workflow
Builds the model and memo but must export the deck through a separate tool
Requires the CSV to be reformatted before any file can be generated
rationale: The docs show Claude taking one data source and producing coordinated deliverables (spreadsheet model, document, presentation) within a single workflow. The distractors invent format or tooling limits that the feature does not impose.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: An associate is designing an outbound-email workflow around the Gmail connector. Which documented behavior should the design account for?
options: Claude can send email on the user's behalf once the connector is authorized
Claude can schedule email to send later but not send it immediately
Claude cannot send email; at most it helps compose a draft a person reviews and sends
Claude reads inbox context but cannot help compose any message
rationale: The connector keeps a human checkpoint on outbound mail: Claude works with email context but does not send messages, so a person sends. Options claiming autonomous or scheduled sending overstate the connector's abilities; claiming no composition help understates them.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: A team lead wants every new hire to produce expert-level output on a recurring, standardized task from day one, without re-explaining the team's method each time. The documented mechanism is:
options: Custom instructions the hire re-enters at the start of every conversation
A Project containing example outputs the hire copies and adapts manually
A detailed prompt template the hire pastes before each task
A Skill that packages the team's proven approach and applies automatically when relevant
rationale: Skills package institutional knowledge and proven workflows so anyone, including new members, gets consistent expert-level results without re-explaining the standard, and they apply automatically when relevant. The alternatives all rely on the hire manually supplying the method each time.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Skills

stem: An associate wants a meeting-prep routine where Claude reads today's calendar, finds the relevant email thread, and pulls a briefing document from Drive. The documented enabler is:
options: Connecting Google Calendar, Gmail, and Drive so Claude can gather that context on request
Uploading screenshots of the calendar, email, and brief into each chat
Enabling extended thinking so Claude can infer the meeting details
Storing last week's notes in a Project knowledge base
rationale: Google Workspace connectors let Claude read calendar events, search email, and retrieve Drive files from the conversation, accessing the data only when asked, which is exactly what meeting prep needs. Screenshots, extended thinking, and stored notes cannot retrieve live calendar, email, and Drive content on demand.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: A solutions architect is explaining how MCP connectors and Skills relate within a single workflow. The documentation positions them as:
options: Interchangeable capabilities that provide the same underlying functionality
Independent tracks, with connectors intended for consumers and Skills for developers
Complementary: connectors provide access to tools and data; Skills encode the procedure for using them
Layered: a Skill must be installed before any connector will function
rationale: MCP connects Claude to external services and data, while Skills supply procedural knowledge; used together, connectors provide access and a Skill standardizes how to complete the task. They are neither identical, audience-segmented, nor dependent on one another to function.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Skills

stem: An associate must produce a business proposal that requires careful up-front planning as well as broad, well-sourced background research. Which documented combination does Claude's guidance recommend?
options: Web search alone, run separately for each subtopic in the proposal
Extended thinking to plan the approach, combined with Research to gather and synthesize cited sources
A Project plus an incognito chat to keep working drafts separate
Memory enabled so Claude recalls and reuses prior proposals automatically
rationale: The docs recommend pairing extended thinking (to reason through the plan) with Research (to gather and synthesize sourced information) for tasks like proposals and multi-paper analyses. Repeated single searches, project/incognito setup, and memory do not together provide both planning depth and cited multi-source research.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Web search, extended thinking, and research

stem: A user starts a long Research task and asks whether they must stay on the page while it runs. Per the documentation, the user:
options: Must keep the tab open and confirm each search step as it runs
Can only run Research if no other Claude activity happens meanwhile
Receives the finished report only by email once processing completes
Can navigate away or close the tab and return later to a finished, cited report
rationale: Research works agentically in the background, so the user can navigate away or close the tab and come back to a structured, cited report. It does not require step-by-step confirmation, block other work, or deliver results solely by email.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: A recurring cross-functional initiative spans many chats and several teammates, and its shared context keeps drifting between conversations. The documented structure to keep that context consistent is:
options: A Project bundling shared instructions and knowledge that all of its chats inherit
One continuously growing conversation that everyone appends to
A Skill each teammate installs individually before starting work
Custom instructions each teammate configures in their own account settings
rationale: Projects bundle shared instructions and a knowledge base that every chat in the project inherits, and on Team/Enterprise they can be shared with collaborators, keeping recurring work consistent. A single mega-conversation, per-user Skills, or individually configured custom instructions do not provide shared, inherited project context.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: A manager notes that file creation shifts Claude from an advisor that only describes work to a collaborator that produces it. Per the documentation, this is because Claude can:
options: Summarize the required steps for the associate to carry out manually
Generate a fill-in template the associate completes offline
Write and run code in a sandbox to produce the actual files and analyses
Describe the intended deliverable in detail without generating a file
