rationale: By writing and running code in a sandbox, Claude produces real files and analyses, making it an active collaborator: you bring context and strategy, Claude handles implementation. The other options keep Claude in an advisory, describe-only role, which is what file creation moves beyond.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: A task spans several areas that map to different Skills. The documentation says Skills 'combine' or 'stack,' which in practice means Claude:
options: Prompts the user to select which Skill applies before each step
Automatically identifies the relevant Skills and coordinates their use
Applies Skills strictly one at a time in the order they were created
Limits a single task to only the most recently used Skill
rationale: Skills are composable: for a multi-step task Claude automatically identifies which Skills are needed and coordinates them, without the user selecting each one. The distractors describe manual selection or artificial one-Skill-at-a-time limits that contradict the composability the docs describe.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Skills

stem: A stakeholder asks the team to 'fully automate' approvals so Claude finalizes them without review. Which design best aligns with Anthropic's documented guidance on responsible integration?
options: Remove manual review so approvals complete without delay
Grant Claude send permissions so it can finalize routine approvals itself
Route only exceptions to a human while Claude auto-approves the remainder
Keep a human checkpoint before consequential actions, e.g., Claude drafts and a person approves
rationale: Responsible integration retains a human checkpoint before consequential actions, reinforced by documented limits such as Claude drafting but not sending email, so a person stays accountable. Removing review, granting autonomous finalization, or auto-approving the majority all eliminate the checkpoint the guidance preserves.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: A user launches Research from a vague prompt that omits key scope. Per the documentation, Claude may:
options: Pause and ask follow-up or scoping questions to narrow the investigation
Proceed on its own assumptions and flag them only at the end of the report
Return a shorter report limited strictly to the words in the prompt
Decline the request until a complete brief is provided
rationale: When the brief lacks scope, Research can ask scoping questions before it begins so the investigation is targeted and effort is not wasted on a vague prompt. It does not silently assume, arbitrarily truncate, or refuse outright.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: An admin is writing the instructions for a shared Project. Per the documentation, project instructions perform best when they contain:
options: Every task-specific step that any future chat in the project might need
A full transcript of an ideal conversation for Claude to imitate
General context, key guidelines, and Claude's role, leaving task specifics to each chat
The knowledge-base files re-pasted as text to improve reliability
rationale: The docs advise keeping project instructions concise, covering general context, key guidelines, and Claude's role, while task-specific detail belongs in the individual chat. Packing in every task step, a full transcript, or re-pasted files bloats the instructions and works against the recommended approach.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: A Project has accumulated many files over months and responses feel less focused. To keep it performing well, the documentation recommends periodically:
options: Adding every potentially relevant file so nothing is missing
Removing files that are no longer actively used to preserve available context
Converting the knowledge files into project instructions
Starting a fresh project each time the current one fills up
rationale: Regularly removing unused project files maximizes available context and keeps the project focused on currently relevant knowledge. Adding more files, moving them into instructions, or abandoning the project do not address context bloat the way cleanup does.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: An individual on a Pro plan wants to turn on code execution and file creation. Per the documentation, where is this done?
options: It is always on for Free/Pro/Max plans and cannot be changed
Per conversation, by attaching a file before Claude can run code
Under Settings > Connectors, by adding a sandbox connector
Under Settings > Capabilities, by enabling 'Code execution and file creation'
rationale: For individual Free/Pro/Max plans, the capability is enabled under Settings > Capabilities via 'Code execution and file creation,' which also powers artifacts and Skills. It is not always-on, not a per-file toggle, and not found under Connectors.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: For a newly created Enterprise organization, the documentation states that code execution and file creation is enabled by default with:
options: 'Allow network egress' toggled off by default
Network egress enabled by default so all features work fully
The capability off by default until an admin enables it per user
Access restricted to Skills authored by Anthropic
rationale: For new Enterprise orgs the capability is on by default but with 'Allow network egress' toggled off, the most secure baseline, so nothing leaves the sandbox until an admin changes it. It is not on with egress open, not off by default, and not limited to Anthropic-authored Skills.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: On a Team plan, members report they cannot authenticate the Google Workspace connectors. Per the documentation, what must happen first?
options: Each member authorizes the connector individually with no admin step required
An admin must install the connector on every member's desktop app
An Owner or Primary Owner must enable the connectors at the organization level
Members must request access through Anthropic support
rationale: On Team and Enterprise plans, an Owner or Primary Owner enables Google Workspace connectors at the organization level before individual members can authenticate them. Members cannot self-enable, no per-device install is required, and no support request is involved.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: A user asks which plans can use the Google Workspace connectors (Gmail, Calendar, Drive). Per the documentation, they are available to:
options: Enterprise plans only, through a dedicated admin console
All users on Claude and Claude Desktop, with org-level enablement required on Team/Enterprise
Pro and Max plans only, after activating a paid add-on
Team and Enterprise plans only, once seats are assigned
rationale: Google Workspace connectors are available to all users on Claude and Claude Desktop, with Team and Enterprise requiring org-level enablement first. The other options wrongly restrict availability to specific paid tiers.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: A team member wants Claude to automatically apply Anthropic's built-in document Skills when generating a formatted spreadsheet. Which setting must be enabled for Skills to run?
options: Extended thinking
Code execution
The web search tool
Incognito mode
rationale: Skills execute in the code-execution environment, so code execution must be enabled for built-in document skills to invoke automatically. Extended thinking and web search are unrelated capabilities, and incognito mode restricts features rather than enabling Skills.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Skills

stem: An admin has authored a custom Skill as a folder containing a SKILL.md file. According to the documentation, how is it added to Claude?
options: Paste the SKILL.md contents into a project's custom instructions
Email the folder to Anthropic support for provisioning
Compress the folder into a ZIP file and upload it under Customize > Skills
Attach the folder to a chat and ask Claude to install it
rationale: The documented flow is to package the skill folder as a ZIP and upload it under Customize > Skills. Pasting into instructions, emailing support, or attaching in a chat are not the provisioning path.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Skills

stem: An enterprise wants a set of Skills to be available and consistent for every associate without individual setup. What does the documentation say Team/Enterprise Owners can do?
options: Provision skills organization-wide so they appear for every user
Require each member to rebuild the same skill locally
Share skills only by exporting and importing chat transcripts
Embed the skill files inside a shared project as attachments
rationale: Owners on Team/Enterprise can provision skills org-wide, and they appear for all users with a team indicator — ensuring consistency without per-user setup. The other options are neither documented nor scalable.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Skills

stem: An organization is enabling code execution and wants the most secure starting configuration. Which baseline does the documentation recommend?
options: Enable full network access so Claude can validate outputs online
Disable the code sandbox to reduce the attack surface
Grant access to all trusted domains from the outset
Begin with network access turned off, then expand as trust grows
rationale: The recommended cautious baseline is network access off — Claude can still run code and create files but cannot transmit data externally — expanding to trusted domains later. Disabling the sandbox or opening full/broad access increases risk.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: A source policy document referenced by a project has been superseded by a new version. What is the documented maintenance step?
options: Create a brand-new project for the updated policy
Update or replace the outdated file in the project's knowledge
Add the new version while keeping the old file for history
Paste the changes into a single chat and pin it for reference
rationale: Project knowledge grounds every chat, so updating or replacing the outdated file keeps responses aligned with current policy. Keeping the old file risks stale references, and a new project or pinned chat doesn't refresh the grounding.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are projects

stem: An associate is unsure what data a Google Workspace connector exposes to Claude. What does the documentation state about connector access?
options: Claude can surface workspace data the user lacks rights to view
Access requires sharing the user's account password in the chat
Claude mirrors the user's existing permissions and cannot reach anything they can't
Connectors grant Claude broad read access independent of the user's roles
rationale: Claude mirrors the user's existing permissions and retrieves data only when asked; it cannot access anything the user can't already access. No password is shared, and access is never broader than the user's own.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: The documentation notes that tools and connectors are token-intensive. What practical benefit follows from managing which ones are enabled?
options: Enabling only the connectors you need preserves context-window space and optimizes usage limits
Enabling every connector improves answer quality regardless of token cost
Connectors carry no token cost once they have been authorized
Disabling unused connectors has no measurable effect on available context
rationale: Because tools and connectors consume tokens, enabling only what's needed preserves context-window space and optimizes usage limits. The remaining options misstate the token cost or its impact.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: Where does a custom Skill's instructions reside, according to the documentation?
options: In the project's description field
In an account-level config.json manifest
Embedded within a PDF that Claude parses at runtime
In a SKILL.md Markdown file, with optional scripts and resources
rationale: A skill is a folder whose SKILL.md Markdown file holds the instructions, with optional scripts and resources for advanced functionality. The other locations are not where skill instructions live.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Skills

stem: Anthropic's built-in document Skills provide enhanced creation for which file types?
options: Plain-text and Markdown files only
Excel, Word, PowerPoint, and PDF files
Image and video files
HTML pages and CSS stylesheets
rationale: Built-in Anthropic skills enhance creation of Excel, Word, PowerPoint, and PDF files and invoke automatically when relevant (with code execution enabled). The other sets are not what the built-in document skills cover.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Skills

stem: Which of the following help maximize a project's available context window? (Select THREE.)
options: Keep project instructions concise and focused
Remove files the project no longer needs
Rely on the project's retrieval to load only relevant content
Upload every potentially related file so nothing is missing
Write exhaustive, maximally detailed instructions
rationale: Concise instructions, removing unused files, and leaning on retrieval (RAG) to load only relevant content all preserve context. Uploading everything or writing exhaustive instructions consumes context and does the opposite.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: On a consumer plan (Free, Pro, or Max), which setting controls whether a user's chats are used to train future models?
options: The usage dashboard in account settings
A per-project data-retention control
The 'Help improve Claude' toggle in Privacy Settings
Setting the account to open new chats in Incognito by default
rationale: The 'Help improve Claude' toggle in Privacy Settings governs whether consumer chats and coding sessions are used for model improvement. The usage dashboard, a project control, and an incognito default do not set the training choice.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center — Model improvement privacy settings

stem: A consumer user switches the model-improvement setting off. What does the documentation say this does?
options: New chats and coding sessions won't be used for future training, but data already in a training run cannot be retracted
All past conversations are immediately deleted from Anthropic's systems
Data is retroactively removed from models that were already trained
The change takes effect only after the current billing cycle ends
rationale: Turning it off is forward-looking: future training won't use new conversations, but data already in a started training run or an already-trained model can't be pulled back. It does not delete history, retroactively scrub models, or wait for a billing cycle.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center — Model improvement privacy settings

stem: Even with model improvement turned off, under what circumstance does the documentation say a conversation may still be used?
options: When the conversation is unusually long
When the user is on a Free plan
When the chat references a competing product
When trust-and-safety classifiers flag it for harmful content or policy enforcement
rationale: Opting out does not exempt flagged content: conversations flagged by trust-and-safety classifiers may still be reviewed and used to improve safety systems and enforce policy. Length, plan, or competitor mentions are not triggers.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center — Model improvement privacy settings

stem: What does the Privacy Center say about incognito chats and model training?
options: They may be used for training when model improvement is enabled
They are never used to improve Claude, even if model improvement is enabled
They are used for training only on Team and Enterprise plans
They are automatically deleted after 24 hours
rationale: Incognito chats are never used to improve Claude, even when model improvement is enabled — making them suitable for sensitive, one-off conversations. The other statements misstate this guarantee.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center — Is my data used for model training?

stem: When model-improvement data is used, which of the following does the Privacy Center say is NOT included?
options: The text of the conversation itself
The custom style or preferences applied to the chat
Raw connector or MCP server content, unless copied directly into the chat
Feedback the user submits on a response
rationale: Training data may include the conversation, custom styles, preferences, and submitted feedback, but not raw connector or MCP content unless that content is directly copied into the chat.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center — Is my data used for model training?

stem: By default, which offerings does the documentation state are NOT used for model training?
options: Commercial offerings — Team, Enterprise, the API, and government — are not used by default
All Free-plan consumer conversations are excluded by default
Only Pro-plan conversations are excluded by default
No conversations on any plan are used unless the user opts in
rationale: Claude for Work (Team/Enterprise), the API, and government offerings are not used for training by default; the opt-in/out training choice applies to consumer plans (Free, Pro, Max). The other options misstate which plans are excluded.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center — Is my data used for model training?

stem: A user submits thumbs up/down feedback on a Claude response. How does the Privacy Center say the related conversation is retained?
options: It is stored for 24 hours and then purged
Only the rated message is kept, not the surrounding conversation
The feedback is anonymized and immediately discarded
The related conversation is stored in Anthropic's secured back-end for up to five years
rationale: Submitting thumbs up/down stores the entire related conversation — including content, custom styles, and preferences — in Anthropic's secured back-end for up to five years. The shorter retention and message-only options are incorrect.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center — Is my data used for model training?

stem: What does the documentation state about Google Workspace connector data and model training?
options: Anthropic trains models on Gmail, Drive, and Calendar connector data by default
Anthropic does not train models on Gmail, Drive, or Calendar connector data
Connector data is shared with third parties for analytics
Connector data is retained indefinitely regardless of whether the chat is deleted
rationale: Anthropic does not train on Gmail, Drive, or Calendar connector data. Retrieved data is stored with the chat, so deleting the chat deletes that data — it is not retained indefinitely or shared externally.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: The file-creation feature can grant Claude limited internet access. What does the documentation advise associates working with sensitive data to do?
options: Treat the risk as negligible since files are generated locally
Route all sensitive work through a separate account
Monitor chats closely with sensitive data, or disable network egress
Disable the code sandbox before uploading sensitive files
rationale: Because the feature can give Claude limited internet access, Anthropic advises monitoring chats with sensitive data; disabling network egress removes the external-transmission risk. Downplaying the risk or disabling the sandbox is not the guidance.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: An associate needs to analyze a dataset containing regulated personal identifiers. Which approach is consistent with responsible-use guidance?
options: Anonymize or redact identifiers first, then follow org policy and least-access handling
Upload it unchanged because internal data is exempt from safeguards
Rely on instructing Claude not to retain the data and proceed as-is
Defer the analysis until the data is no longer regulated
rationale: Applying data-sensitivity safeguards means anonymizing or redacting regulated identifiers before use and following policy and least-access handling; connectors likewise retrieve only the minimum needed and mirror permissions. Treating internal data as exempt or relying on a retention instruction does not satisfy the safeguards.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: A compliance analyst asks what happens to the information Claude pulled in through a connector once the underlying chat is deleted. According to the documentation, deleting that chat will:
options: Move the retrieved data to a separate connector cache until the connector is disconnected
Anonymize the retrieved data and retain it for product analytics
Delete the data retrieved during that chat, because it is stored together with the conversation
Leave the retrieved data in Claude's memory while hiding it from history
rationale: Connector-retrieved data is stored with its associated chat, so deleting the chat deletes that retrieved data. The distractors invent a separate cache, an analytics retention path, and a memory carryover, none of which the documentation describes.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: A security reviewer on an Enterprise plan asks how incognito chats are handled. Which statement is accurate?
options: They are retained for a limited period for safety and are included in organizational exports and the Compliance API
They are permanently deleted the moment the window is closed and never appear in exports
They are excluded from the Compliance API but retained indefinitely for the individual user
They are stored only on the user's device and never reach Anthropic's servers
rationale: Although incognito chats aren't saved to personal history, on Team/Enterprise they are retained briefly for safety and appear in organizational exports and the Compliance API, so they are not a hard non-retention guarantee. The distractors overstate deletion, misstate Compliance API coverage, and invent local-only storage.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use incognito chats

stem: An associate needs to ask Claude about a sensitive, one-time matter that they do not want personalized on or retained. Why is an incognito chat an appropriate choice?
options: It applies stronger end-to-end encryption than a standard chat
It keeps the conversation out of chat history and memory, and out of model training
It raises the usage limit for that session
It routes the request to a dedicated compliance model
rationale: Incognito chats aren't saved to history or memory and are never used for training, which suits sensitive one-off conversations. The distractors invent an encryption difference, a usage benefit, and a separate model that the documentation does not describe.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use incognito chats

stem: Which statements align with Anthropic's privacy documentation? (Select TWO.)
options: Incognito chats are never used to improve Claude
Team, Enterprise, and API data are not used for model training by default
Opting out of training retroactively removes your data from already-trained models
Raw content pulled by connectors is always used for training
rationale: Incognito data is never used to improve Claude, and commercial (Team/Enterprise/API) data is not used for training by default. Opting out is forward-looking only, and connector raw content is not used for training unless copied into the chat.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center — Is my data used for model training?

stem: An associate turns Research on, but Claude replies almost instantly without an in-depth, multi-step investigation. What should they verify first?
options: That memory has been cleared for the project
That web search is enabled, since Research depends on it, and that Research is actually active for the message
That the conversation is running in incognito mode
That extended thinking has been turned off
rationale: Research requires web search to be on and the Research indicator to be active; a shallow answer usually means one of those isn't engaged. Memory, incognito, and extended thinking are unrelated to whether Research runs.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: An associate reports that Claude will not produce downloadable files for them. What is the documented first step?
options: Upgrade the organization to the Enterprise plan
Enable Code execution and file creation under Settings > Capabilities
Disable web search to free up resources for file generation
Recreate the request inside a new project
rationale: File creation requires Code execution and file creation to be enabled under Settings > Capabilities (or organization settings on Team/Enterprise). The distractors describe an unnecessary upgrade and unrelated toggles.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: An associate reaches their usage limit partway through a task. According to the documentation, what are their options?
options: Wait for the five-hour reset, upgrade the plan, or, on paid plans, use usage credits
Wait for the monthly billing cycle to roll over before continuing
Clear chat history to reset the usage counter immediately
Switch to a different browser to obtain a fresh limit
rationale: Usage sessions reset every five hours; alternatively the user can upgrade or, on Pro/Max, purchase and use usage credits to continue at standard API rates. Billing cycles, clearing history, and changing browsers do not reset usage.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: An associate keeps hitting a length limit inside one very long conversation. What is the documented remedy?
options: Continue adding messages to the same conversation
Start a new conversation, or use a project to work with larger amounts of information more efficiently
Upgrade to Enterprise, which removes length limits entirely
Turn off memory to shorten the context
rationale: Length limits cap a single conversation; the fix is to start a new chat or move to a project (RAG) to handle larger information efficiently. Adding more to the same thread compounds the problem, Enterprise does not remove the cap, and disabling memory does not address conversation length.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: A Google Workspace connector returns a 'you may need to reconnect' error. What does the documentation advise?
options: Reinstall Claude and clear the browser cache
Under Customize > Connectors, disconnect the Google connector and re-authenticate the next time a Workspace feature is used
Delete all chats that referenced the connector
Disable code execution and retry the request
rationale: The documented remedy is to disconnect the Google connector under Customize > Connectors and re-authenticate on next use (removing the connection in Google account settings if needed). The distractors describe unrelated actions that do not re-establish the connector authorization.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: A workflow produces accurate results but consumes usage quickly on routine steps. Which documented optimization helps?
options: Route every task to Opus for maximum quality
Use efficient models such as Haiku or the latest Sonnet for most work, reserving Opus for the hardest tasks
Add more connectors so the work is distributed across them
Lengthen every prompt with additional context
rationale: The guidance is to use efficient models (Haiku or the most recent Sonnet) for most tasks at lower token cost, keeping Opus for genuinely complex work. Always using Opus, adding connectors, or padding prompts increases rather than reduces consumption.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Manage usage credits

stem: A custom Skill is not being invoked when the associate expects it to. Consistent with the documentation, what should they check first?
options: That the skill's description clearly states when it should be used, and that code execution is enabled
That the skill's files are each under 30MB in size
That the skill has already been shared with the entire organization
That memory is disabled so the skill can load
rationale: Skills load via progressive disclosure based on their description, so a vague description is the common reason one does not fire; Skills also require code execution. File size, org sharing, and memory state do not govern whether a skill activates.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: A long single conversation is starting to lose track of earlier context. Beyond starting fresh, what structural optimization does the documentation suggest?
options: Attaching all potentially relevant files to the current chat
Moving the work into a project, where RAG surfaces only the relevant knowledge
Disabling web search to conserve context
Renaming the conversation to reset its context window
rationale: Projects use RAG to work with larger amounts of information efficiently, which helps when a single conversation is losing earlier context. Piling on files consumes context, and disabling web search or renaming the chat does nothing to expand available context.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: A user wants consistently strong output from Claude. Which practices reflect documented prompt-engineering guidance? (Select THREE.)
options: Give clear, explicit instructions that state the task, audience, and desired format
Provide one or more examples of the desired output
Assign a relevant role or persona to Claude
Keep wording deliberately vague so Claude has room to interpret
Omit the output format so Claude can choose freely
rationale: Explicit instructions, examples (multishot), and role prompting are all documented techniques. Deliberate vagueness and omitting the desired format remove the signal Claude needs and reduce quality.
referenceText: Claude Docs — Prompt engineering best practices

stem: Which statements about Artifacts are accurate per the documentation? (Select TWO.)
options: They are created for substantial, self-contained content, typically more than about 15 lines
They support a version selector and in-place editing for Markdown
Sharing an artifact requires pasting an API key
They are available only on the Enterprise plan
rationale: Artifacts are generated for substantial, self-contained, reusable content and support versions and in-place Markdown editing. Sharing needs no API key, and Artifacts are available across plans, not Enterprise-only.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are artifacts and how do I use them

stem: Before sharing a Claude-generated spreadsheet externally, which steps does the documentation support? (Select TWO.)
options: Review the formulas and underlying data for accuracy
Check the file for any sensitive content
Treat the file as final because Claude produced it
Delete the file without opening it
rationale: Generated files are a strong starting point, so the guidance is to verify formulas and data and review sensitive content before sharing. Assuming finality or discarding the file unread both contradict that guidance.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: Which statements about code execution and file creation are accurate? (Select THREE.)
options: It is available on all plans, from Free through Enterprise
The maximum file size is 30MB per file
It runs in a private, sandboxed computing environment
It can generate native video files
It requires a separate paid add-on on every plan
rationale: The feature is available on all plans, caps files at 30MB each, and runs in a private sandbox. It does not produce native video and requires no separate paid add-on.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Create and edit files with Claude

stem: Which practices help maximize the context available within a conversation? (Select THREE.)
options: Use projects, whose RAG loads only the relevant content
Keep project instructions concise
Turn off extended thinking when it is not needed
Attach every file you might conceivably need
Avoid ever starting a new conversation
rationale: Projects (RAG), concise instructions, and turning off extended thinking when unneeded all preserve context. Attaching every file and refusing to start fresh consume context and work against the goal.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — How usage and length limits work

stem: Which statements about the Research feature are true? (Select TWO.)
options: It requires web search to be turned on
It synthesizes multiple sources into a cited report
It runs even when web search is turned off
It never draws on your connected apps
rationale: Research requires web search to be on and produces a cited, multi-source report that can draw on both the web and connected internal context such as Gmail, Calendar, and Docs. It cannot run without web search and does use connected apps.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use research on Claude

stem: Which statements about the Google Workspace connectors are true? (Select THREE.)
options: Claude mirrors your existing access permissions
Gmail can draft messages but not send them
On Team or Enterprise, an Owner must enable the connectors organization-wide first
Claude can reach files you personally do not have access to
Connector data is used to train Anthropic's models by default
rationale: Connectors mirror the user's existing permissions, can draft but not send email, and require org-level enablement by an Owner on Team/Enterprise. Claude cannot exceed the user's access, and connector data is not used for training by default.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — Use Google Workspace connectors

stem: Which statements about Skills are accurate? (Select THREE.)
options: They require code execution to be enabled
A skill is a folder that contains a SKILL.md file
The built-in Office and PDF skills invoke automatically when relevant
Custom skills are shared with everyone in the organization by default
rationale: Skills require code execution, are defined by a folder containing a SKILL.md file, and the built-in document skills auto-invoke when relevant. Custom skills are private unless explicitly shared, not shared by default.
referenceText: Claude Help Center — What are skills

stem: A privacy officer is briefing staff on how Anthropic handles their conversation data. Which statements are accurate? (Select THREE.)
options: Incognito chats are never used to improve Claude
Team, Enterprise, and API inputs and outputs are not used to train models by default
Content pulled in through a connector is not used for training unless it is copied into the conversation
Opting out of training removes your prior data from models that have already been trained
Thumbs up / thumbs down feedback is deleted immediately and never retained
rationale: Correct: incognito chats are excluded from training; commercial offerings (Team, Enterprise, API) are not used for training by default; and connector content is only in scope if the user copies it into the chat. Distractor 3 is wrong because opting out is forward-looking only and cannot un-train an existing model. Distractor 4 is wrong because feedback is retained (up to five years) to improve the service, not discarded immediately.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center - Is my data used for model training?

stem: A user starts an incognito chat in the Claude app. Which two statements accurately describe how incognito chats behave? (Select TWO.)
options: They are not saved to your chat history or used in memory
They are never used to improve Claude
They raise your usage limit for that session
They can be reopened and continued later from your history
They can be shared with teammates through a conversation link
rationale: Correct: incognito chats are not stored in history or memory and are never used for training. Distractor 2 is wrong because incognito status does not change usage limits. Distractor 3 is wrong because, not being saved, they cannot be reopened. Distractor 4 is wrong because there is no saved conversation to share via link.
referenceText: Claude Help Center - Use incognito chats

stem: A marketing manager asks Claude, "Write something about our product," and is disappointed that the draft is generic and misses the point. Based on Anthropic's guidance for getting the best results, what is the single most effective change to the prompt?
options: Add specific context and success criteria, such as the audience, the product's key benefit, the desired tone, and the length
Generate several drafts from the same short request and pick whichever one reads best
Ask Claude to make the writing more professional, higher quality, and polished
Switch to a more capable model and resend the identical request unchanged
rationale: Anthropic's guidance stresses being clear and direct and supplying the context Claude needs - audience, goal, tone, and constraints. The vague prompt lacks that information, so adding it is the highest-leverage fix. Generating more drafts from the same underspecified prompt reproduces the same ambiguity; vague quality adjectives give Claude nothing actionable; and a stronger model still gets the same missing context, so the output stays generic.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center - How can I get the best results from Claude?

stem: Claude produces a confident-sounding summary of a research report and cites several specific statistics. Before including these figures in a board presentation, what is the most responsible next step?
options: Verify the statistics against the original source, since Claude can state inaccurate information confidently
Include the figures as-is because Claude cited specific numbers and sounded confident
Ask Claude the same question again and accept the figures if the numbers stay the same
Ask Claude to rate its own confidence and include the figures if it reports being highly confident
rationale: Anthropic advises that Claude can make mistakes or hallucinate and that users should verify important facts against reliable sources. Confidence, specificity, and self-reported confidence are not evidence of accuracy, and re-asking may simply reproduce the same error. Only checking against the primary source establishes whether the figures are correct.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center - How can I get the best results from Claude?

stem: A consultant wants a Claude Project so that every conversation inside it automatically follows the same guidelines and can draw on the same background materials. Which two actions support this goal? (Select 2.)
options: Add the relevant background documents to the Project's knowledge so conversations can reference them
Set custom instructions for the Project describing the tone, format, and rules Claude should follow
Paste the full guidelines and documents again at the start of every message to be safe
Rename each new chat with the client's name so Claude applies the guidelines automatically
Delete each conversation after use so the Project knowledge refreshes for the next chat
rationale: Projects let you add knowledge (documents and context) that all chats in the Project can reference and set custom instructions that apply across those chats. Re-pasting everything each message defeats the purpose of a Project; renaming a chat has no effect on Claude's behavior; and deleting conversations neither refreshes nor maintains Project knowledge, it only loses history.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center - What are Projects?

stem: An HR coordinator plans to paste a spreadsheet of employees' full names, home addresses, and Social Security numbers into Claude to reformat it into a table. According to Anthropic's responsible-use guidance, what is the best course of action?
options: Keep the real identifiers out of the prompt and reformat using de-identified or sample data instead
Submit the full data set, since reformatting does not analyze the identifiers
Submit the data after instructing Claude not to store or remember any of it
Submit only a subset of the columns, since partial personal data is not considered sensitive
rationale: Anthropic's guidance is to be thoughtful about sharing sensitive personal information and avoid submitting data you would not want processed. De-identifying or using sample data completes the formatting task without exposing real identifiers. A task being low-risk does not reduce the data's sensitivity, asking Claude to 'not remember' is not a valid data-handling control, and submitting a subset still exposes real personal data.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center - Is my data used for model training? (guidance on sensitive personal data)

stem: A user wants Claude to review a 60-page contract, cross-reference several attached policy documents, and reason carefully through complex clauses in a single pass. When choosing how to approach this in the Claude app, which consideration is most aligned with Anthropic's guidance?
options: Select a more capable model suited to complex, multi-step reasoning and provide the documents as context
Choose the fastest, lightest model, since response speed is the priority for legal review
Send only the instruction 'review this contract' without attaching any of the documents
Choose based on price alone, since model capability does not affect the quality of the analysis
rationale: Anthropic offers models that balance speed and capability and recommends matching the model to the task; demanding multi-step analysis benefits from a more capable model plus the needed context. The fastest model is not automatically right for hard reasoning, omitting the documents removes essential context, and capability does meaningfully affect output quality, so price alone is the wrong basis.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center - What are the differences between Claude models?

stem: An analyst's first prompt returned an answer that was close but too long and lacked citations. Following prompt-refinement and validation best practices, which two follow-up actions are appropriate? (Select 2.)
options: Ask Claude to shorten the response to a specific length and to list the sources it relied on
Provide a short example of the exact format and level of detail you want the answer to match
Tell Claude the previous answer was wrong and demand it never make mistakes again
Start a brand-new chat with the same vague prompt and hope for a better result
Instruct Claude to guarantee its citations are correct so you can skip checking them
rationale: Iterating with clearer instructions (specifying length, asking for sources) and showing an example of the desired output are core techniques Anthropic recommends. Demanding perfection does not improve results, restarting with the same vague prompt repeats the original problem, and a 'guarantee' that removes the need to check citations contradicts the guidance to verify facts, since Claude can fabricate references.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center - How can I get the best results from Claude?

stem: A marketing manager asks Claude to summarize a 40-page industry report she pasted in, and the summary includes a specific market-size dollar figure she does not recognize. Before putting that figure into a board presentation, what is the most responsible next step?
options: Trust the figure because Claude was working directly from the document she provided
Ask Claude to point to where in the pasted report the statistic appears, then confirm it against the source text
Rephrase the request as a direct command so Claude is forced to be accurate
Add a custom instruction telling Claude to never make mistakes
rationale: Claude can misattribute or fabricate specifics even when a source is provided, so the reliable check is to have it locate the claim in the source and then verify it against the original text. Trusting it because a document was supplied ignores that hallucinations still occur; commanding phrasing does not improve factual accuracy; and an instruction to 'never make mistakes' cannot guarantee correctness.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center - Claude is providing incorrect or misleading responses

stem: You want Claude to draft a short brief on a recent regulatory change and you plan to fact-check it afterward. Which prompt instruction best supports your ability to validate the output?
options: Ask Claude to write the brief as confidently as possible so it reads authoritatively
Ask Claude to include, for each key claim, the source it relied on and to flag where it is unsure or lacks a reliable source
Tell Claude that accuracy is extremely important and repeat that instruction three times
Ask Claude to keep the brief under 100 words so there is less content to check
rationale: Asking Claude to cite sources for claims and flag its uncertainty gives you concrete anchors to verify and surfaces the weakest points, which is the core of output validation. Maximizing confidence hides uncertainty, repeating an instruction does not make facts correct, and shortening the text reduces volume but not the risk that a remaining claim is wrong.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center - Claude is providing incorrect or misleading responses

stem: An analyst is reviewing a Claude-generated summary of a legal case and wants to judge which parts may be hallucinations worth verifying. Which two signs warrant extra scrutiny before relying on the content? (Select 2.)
options: A precise citation, case name, or quotation the analyst cannot find in the provided documents
A statistic or date stated with high confidence but with no source given
A section where Claude explicitly says it is unsure and recommends confirming
A summary that restates points already present in the source the analyst supplied
rationale: Unverifiable specific citations and confidently stated facts with no source are classic hallucination-risk signals and should be checked against primary sources. A passage where Claude flags its own uncertainty is transparent and appropriately cautious, and a summary that faithfully restates the supplied source is the low-risk behavior you want, so neither is a red flag.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center - Claude is providing incorrect or misleading responses

stem: A user keeps getting overly generic email drafts from Claude. Their prompt reads: 'Write a good email to the client.' Applying the principle of being clear and direct, which revision is most likely to produce a usable draft?
options: 'Write a really, really good and professional email to the client, please.'
'Write a 120-word email to client Dana declining the March timeline, proposing April 15, and keeping a warm, apologetic tone.'
'You are the world's best copywriter. Write the email.'
'Write an email. Make it perfect and don't leave anything out.'
rationale: Being clear and direct means specifying the concrete details Claude needs - recipient, length, purpose, key points, and tone - which the second option does. Intensifiers like 'really good' or 'perfect' provide no actionable direction, and a role label alone without the specifics still leaves Claude guessing what the email should say.
referenceText: Anthropic prompt engineering - Be clear and direct

stem: A support lead wants Claude to categorize incoming tickets into a fixed set of internal labels and format the result consistently. Which prompting technique is most effective for reliable, correctly formatted output?
options: Provide two or three example tickets with their correct labels and formatting so Claude can match the pattern
Ask Claude to be as creative as possible when choosing labels
Send each ticket with no instructions and correct the output manually every time
Tell Claude to use whatever categories it thinks are best
rationale: Providing a few worked examples (multishot prompting) shows Claude the exact labels and format expected, sharply improving consistency for classification tasks. Encouraging creativity or letting Claude invent categories works against a fixed, consistent scheme, and giving no guidance guarantees continued manual correction.
referenceText: Anthropic prompt engineering - Multishot prompting with examples

stem: A consultant has two needs: a quick, high-volume task of reformatting and lightly rewording dozens of short meeting notes, and a separate dense, multi-step strategic analysis. What is the most sensible way to think about choosing a Claude model for each?
options: Always use the most capable, highest-reasoning model for every task regardless of the work
Match the model to the task: a faster, lighter model for the simple high-volume edits and a more capable model for the complex reasoning
Always use the fastest model, because speed is what matters most in every case
Pick either model at random, since model choice does not affect quality
rationale: Anthropic offers models tuned for different trade-offs, and the practical approach is to match the model to the job - lighter and faster for simple, repetitive edits, and a more capable model for deep multi-step reasoning. Defaulting to the top model everywhere is slower and costlier than needed, always choosing the fastest sacrifices quality on hard tasks, and model choice does affect results.
referenceText: Anthropic - Models overview

stem: An HR specialist wants to use Claude to help draft responses about employee cases. Which two practices reflect responsible, governance-minded use? (Select 2.)
options: Removing or anonymizing names and other identifying personal details before pasting case information
Treating Claude's draft as final and sending it to employees without review
Having a qualified person review Claude's output before it is acted upon or shared
Pasting full employee records so Claude has maximum context, regardless of sensitivity
rationale: Responsible use means minimizing sensitive and personally identifiable data before sharing it and keeping a human in the loop to review outputs before they are acted on, especially for consequential HR matters. Sending drafts unreviewed removes the necessary human check, and pasting full identifiable records maximizes privacy risk rather than reducing it.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center - Is my data used for model training?

stem: A small team notices Claude gives inconsistent tone and formatting across their recurring client-report work, and they keep re-pasting the same background context every session. Which configuration approach best addresses both problems?
options: Create a Project for the client work and add the standing background documents plus custom instructions describing the desired tone and format
Start a brand-new chat for every report and hope the style stays consistent
Ask Claude in each chat to 'remember' the style from previous conversations
Paste all the background material again at the start of every message
rationale: A Project keeps shared knowledge and custom instructions in one place, so Claude consistently applies the desired tone and format and reuses the background without re-pasting. Fresh chats provide no continuity, Claude does not automatically carry memory across separate conversations on request, and re-pasting everything each message is exactly the inefficiency Projects remove.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center - What are Projects?

stem: Claude's first answer to a complex prompt is vague and misses several requirements. What is the most effective troubleshooting step to improve the result?
options: Resubmit the exact same prompt repeatedly until a better answer appears
Give specific feedback in a follow-up, naming what was missing and asking Claude to revise, adding any details or examples it needs
Conclude the task is beyond Claude's ability and abandon it
Shorten the prompt to a single vague sentence so Claude is less likely to get confused
rationale: Iterating with specific feedback - stating exactly what was missing and supplying the needed detail or examples - is the core technique for improving an underperforming prompt. Resubmitting the identical prompt reproduces the same gaps, giving up forgoes an easy fix, and making the prompt vaguer removes the very guidance Claude needs.
referenceText: Anthropic prompt engineering - Overview

stem: A marketing manager types 'Write something about our new product' and gets a vague, generic result. Based on Anthropic's guidance for getting more helpful answers, which change is most likely to improve the output?
options: Provide specific context and explicit instructions: who the audience is, the product's key features, the desired tone, and the exact format (for example, a 150-word LinkedIn post)
Retype the identical prompt several more times until Claude happens to produce a better version
Append 'and make it perfect, high quality, and professional' to the same prompt
Switch to a faster model so the generic response is at least returned more quickly
rationale: Anthropic's guidance says Claude works best with clear, specific instructions and as much relevant context as possible (audience, purpose, tone, format). Re-running the same underspecified prompt adds no missing context, vague superlatives give Claude nothing actionable, and a faster model produces the same generic answer because speed does not address specificity.
referenceText: Claude Help Center - My prompt isn't giving me a helpful answer

stem: A researcher is using Claude to draft a briefing and wants to guard against inaccurate or fabricated content. Which practices reflect Anthropic's guidance for evaluating and validating Claude's outputs? (Select 3.)
options: Ask Claude to cite or quote the specific sources or provided documents its claims are based on, then check those sources yourself
Independently verify high-stakes facts, figures, and citations against authoritative sources before relying on them
Treat Claude's confident, fluent tone as proof the information is correct, since well-written answers are reliable
Recognize that Claude can hallucinate, stating things that sound authoritative but are wrong, especially about events after its training cutoff
Assume any hyperlink Claude generates is valid and skip opening it to confirm it works
rationale: Correct: Claude can produce convincing but mistaken statements and can be unreliable on recent events, so users should ground answers in citable sources, verify high-stakes information, and stay alert to hallucination. A confident or polished tone is not evidence of accuracy, and Claude can generate broken or fabricated links, so those should be checked rather than trusted.
referenceText: Claude Help Center - Claude is providing incorrect or misleading responses

stem: An operations analyst needs Claude to summarize a very high daily volume of short, simple customer-support tickets, where each summary is straightforward and speed and cost matter more than deep reasoning. Which model choice best fits this workload?
options: Choose a faster, lower-cost model such as Haiku, which is well suited to high-volume, lower-complexity work
Always choose the most powerful model (Opus) for every ticket, since a more capable model is strictly better in all cases
Choose whichever model appears first in the model picker, since that order always reflects the best default
Do not select a model and let the length of each response determine which model is used
rationale: Anthropic positions lighter, faster models like Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks where speed and cost efficiency matter, and lets users change the model to match the task. The most powerful model costs more and is meant for genuinely complex reasoning, picker order is not a rule for matching complexity, and model selection is a deliberate user choice, not something inferred from response length.
referenceText: Claude Help Center - Change the model, effort, and thinking settings

stem: A consultant wants Claude to consistently follow her firm's writing style guide and respond from the perspective of a financial-services advisor across many separate chats, without re-pasting the guide every time. Which approach best accomplishes this?
options: Create a Project, upload the style guide to the project's knowledge, add project instructions describing the advisor role and tone, and start her chats inside that Project.
Keep one long-running conversation open and continue that same thread for every piece of work throughout the engagement.
Save the style guide as a reusable text snippet and paste it into the first message of each new chat.
Enable chat history so that Claude automatically recalls and reapplies the style guide from her earlier, unrelated conversations.
rationale: Key (0) is correct: Projects let you upload reference material to a shared knowledge base and set project instructions (such as a specific tone or answering as a particular role) that apply to every chat started in that Project. (1) forces all work into one unwieldy thread and does not scale. (2) still requires manual re-pasting every time, the burden Projects removes. (3) misstates how chat history works; it does not carry context from separate, unrelated chats into new ones.
referenceText: Claude Help Center, 'What are projects?': upload documents to a project's knowledge base and define project instructions that apply across all chats in the project.

stem: An HR specialist plans to use Claude to help draft performance-review summaries. Which practices reflect responsible, privacy-conscious use of Claude for this task? (Select 2.)
options: Minimize or avoid entering unnecessary sensitive personal data, and remove or anonymize identifying details where they are not needed.
Have a qualified person review and validate each draft before it is finalized or acted upon, rather than treating Claude's output as a final decision.
Include each employee's full compensation and disciplinary history in every prompt to give Claude the most complete context.
Rely on Claude to identify and redact any sensitive fields on its own before the summary is produced.
Let Claude make the final rating and personnel recommendation autonomously to reduce human bias.
rationale: Keys (0) and (1) reflect responsible use: limit exposure of sensitive personal data (minimization/anonymization) and keep a human in the loop to validate outputs before consequential use. (2) contradicts data minimization by loading unnecessary sensitive data. (3) is unsafe because the user, not the model, is responsible for what sensitive data is shared. (4) is wrong because high-stakes personnel decisions require human judgment, not autonomous AI decision-making.
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Center, 'How does Anthropic protect the personal data of Claude users?': data is encrypted with least-privilege access; combined with guidance to keep human review over consequential outputs and minimize sensitive inputs.

stem: A marketing manager keeps getting summaries from Claude that are too long and miss the action items she needs. She currently prompts: "Summarize this meeting transcript." Which revision best applies the principle of being clear and direct?
options: "Summarize this meeting transcript in 3 bullet points, then list every decision made and who owns each follow-up action."
"Please make the summary a bit shorter and more focused than usual if you can."
"Summarize this meeting transcript, keeping in mind you are a highly capable assistant."
"Summarize this meeting transcript as thoroughly and completely as possible."
rationale: Being clear and direct means specifying the exact format, length, and content, as if instructing a new colleague. Option 0 names the structure (3 bullets) and required elements (decisions, owners, follow-ups). Option 1 is vague and hedged, giving no concrete target. Option 2 adds flattery that does not clarify the task. Option 3 asks for completeness, which pushes toward the long, unfocused output she wants to avoid.
referenceText: Anthropic — Be clear and direct (prompt engineering)

stem: An HR specialist wants Claude to classify incoming employee questions into consistent categories (Benefits, Payroll, IT, Other) with a consistent output format. Which prompting technique is most effective for reliable, consistently formatted results?
options: Include a few worked examples showing sample questions and exactly how each should be categorized and formatted.
Write detailed written definitions of the four categories but provide no examples of how they are applied.
Increase the model's creativity so it can explore where each question best fits.
Send each question in a separate new chat so Claude starts fresh every time.
rationale: Multishot (few-shot) prompting — pairing several example inputs with the desired outputs — is the recommended way to make classification accurate and consistently formatted, because Claude patterns its responses on the examples. Detailed definitions alone leave format and edge cases underspecified. Encouraging creativity works against fixed, consistent categories. Starting a fresh chat each time removes helpful context and does not improve consistency.
referenceText: Anthropic — Use examples (multishot prompting)

stem: A financial analyst asks Claude for the current quarter's revenue figures for a specific public company, and Claude provides confident-sounding numbers. Before including them in a report to leadership, what is the most responsible next step?
options: Verify each figure against the company's official filings or another authoritative source before using it.
Ask Claude to double-check its own figures and proceed if it confirms them.
Re-run the prompt a second time and use the numbers if both runs produce the same values.
Include the figures with a note that they were generated by Claude, and let readers judge accuracy.
rationale: Claude can produce plausible but incorrect information (hallucinations), and its knowledge has a training cutoff, so current, specific facts must be independently verified against authoritative sources before use. A model's self-check is not independent verification. Re-running can reproduce the same wrong answer, so agreement is not confirmation. Adding a disclaimer does not make unverified figures safe to rely on for leadership reporting.
referenceText: Anthropic — Reduce hallucinations

stem: A consultant is starting a months-long client engagement and wants Claude to consistently apply the client's brand voice, key background documents, and preferred output style across many separate conversations, without re-pasting that context each time. Which Claude feature is designed for this?
options: Create a Project and add the background documents and instructions to its knowledge and custom instructions.
Keep a single continuous conversation open for the entire engagement and never start a new chat.
Save the context in a document and paste it at the top of every new chat.
Rely on chat history so Claude carries the details forward from a previous, unrelated conversation.
rationale: Projects let you group related chats and attach shared knowledge (documents) and custom instructions so Claude applies the same context and style across every conversation in that Project. A single endless chat is unwieldy and does not scale. Pasting context each time is exactly the manual burden Projects removes. Chat history does not automatically carry details from a separate, unrelated conversation into new ones.
referenceText: Anthropic — Using Projects on Claude

stem: An operations team is drafting internal guidelines for using Claude responsibly in everyday knowledge work. Which practices support responsible, governance-aware use? (Select 3.)
options: Keep a human in the loop to review Claude's outputs before they are used in consequential decisions.
Avoid pasting sensitive personal or confidential data that is not necessary for the task.
Verify factual claims and request sources before relying on them.
Treat outputs as final once they are fluent and well-formatted, to reduce review overhead.
Store complete customer records in prompts so every draft is fully personalized.
rationale: Responsible use centers on human review before consequential use, minimizing exposure of unnecessary sensitive data, and validating facts and sources before relying on them. Treating fluent output as final removes the oversight that catches errors and hallucinations. Loading complete customer records into prompts contradicts data minimization and creates privacy risk.
referenceText: Anthropic — Usage Policy

stem: A writer's prompt is producing off-target results: the tone is wrong and the response ignores parts of a long reference document. Which prompt-optimization adjustments are appropriate to try? (Select 2.)
options: Assign Claude a clear role (for example, "You are an editor for a healthcare newsletter") to steer tone and expertise.
Use structure such as headings or XML tags to clearly separate the reference document from the instructions.
Remove the reference document and let Claude rely on its general knowledge instead.
Reduce the level of detail in the instructions so the tone is less constrained.
rationale: Giving Claude a role via a system/role prompt steers tone, style, and domain expertise, and using clear structure (headings or tags) helps Claude distinguish reference material from instructions so it does not overlook parts of a long document. Removing the reference document loses the source content the writer needs Claude to use. Reducing detail removes the guidance needed to hit the target tone.
referenceText: Anthropic — Giving Claude a role with a system prompt

stem: A marketing manager asks Claude, "Write something about our product," and is disappointed that the response is generic and misses key selling points. Which change best reflects the "be clear and direct" principle and is most likely to improve the result?
options: Specify the audience, the desired length and tone, and the three product benefits to emphasize.
Send the same prompt several more times until a better version appears by chance.
Add the instruction "be creative and think outside the box" to the request.
Tell Claude to keep the answer as short as possible so there is less room for error.
rationale: Being clear and direct means providing the specific context and constraints a new coworker would need: audience, length, tone, and the exact points to emphasize (index 0). Re-sending the identical vague prompt relies on luck (index 1). "Be creative" is still vague and supplies no missing specifics (index 2). Forcing brevity does not address the lack of direction and can drop important selling points (index 3).
referenceText: Anthropic — Be clear and direct (prompt engineering)

stem: A user relies on Claude to draft a briefing that cites specific statistics and legal deadlines. What is the most responsible way to treat Claude's output before sending it to stakeholders?
options: Independently verify the facts, figures, and any cited sources before relying on them.
Trust the numbers because Claude expressed them confidently and without hedging.
Assume the output is accurate as long as the writing is fluent and well organized.
Send it immediately and correct any errors only if a stakeholder raises a concern.
rationale: Claude can produce plausible-sounding but incorrect information, so users should not treat it as a single source of truth and should verify facts, figures, and cited sources before relying on them, especially for high-stakes content (index 0). Confident tone, fluency, and organization are not evidence of accuracy (indexes 1 and 2). Waiting for complaints exposes stakeholders to errors that review is meant to catch (index 3).
referenceText: Anthropic — Claude is providing incorrect or misleading responses. What's going on?

stem: A consultant is setting up a Claude Project so the whole team gets consistent help drafting client proposals in the firm's style. Which actions make good use of Projects' configuration features? (Select 2.)
options: Upload the firm's proposal templates and style guide as project knowledge so responses draw on them.
Add custom instructions describing the tone, structure, and formatting proposals should follow.
Store each client's contract and banking details in the project knowledge so proposals are pre-filled.
Expect new chats in this Project to automatically inherit instructions from the team's unrelated projects.
Leave the brand guidelines out and have Claude infer the firm's style on its own.
rationale: Projects let you add reference material as project knowledge and set custom instructions that shape every chat in that project, which is exactly how to enforce a consistent style (indexes 0 and 1). Storing sensitive banking data is inappropriate and unnecessary (index 2). Instructions apply within a project, not across unrelated ones (index 3). Providing the real guidelines is the point of project knowledge; having Claude infer them defeats the purpose (index 4).
referenceText: Anthropic — What are Projects?

stem: An HR specialist wants to reduce the chance that Claude fabricates policy details when answering employee questions about the company handbook. Which prompting approach is most effective?
options: Provide the relevant handbook text and instruct Claude to answer only from it and state when the answer is not covered.
Instruct Claude to rely on its general knowledge of employment law and note where company policy might differ.
Ask Claude to give its best guess whenever the handbook is unclear so employees always get an answer.
Provide the handbook but tell Claude to fill any gaps using standard industry practice.
rationale: Grounding Claude in the actual source document and allowing it to say when something is not covered is a core technique for producing accurate, grounded answers and reducing fabrication (index 0). Relying on general employment-law knowledge invites answers that do not match company-specific policy (index 1). Encouraging best guesses or gap-filling from industry practice pressures Claude to invent details not in the handbook (indexes 2 and 3).
referenceText: Anthropic — Reduce hallucinations (ground responses in provided documents)

stem: A knowledge worker wants Claude to consistently produce customer-support replies that match one specific format and voice. Which prompting techniques directly help achieve that consistency? (Select 2.)
options: Include a few example question-and-answer pairs that show the exact format and tone wanted.
Assign Claude a role, such as "You are a friendly support specialist for our software," to set the voice.
Raise the model's creativity so each reply feels fresh and individually styled.
Describe the desired format only in general terms, without providing a concrete example.
Let Claude choose a different structure for each ticket based on what fits best.
rationale: Providing example input-output pairs (few-shot/multishot prompting) shows Claude the exact format and tone to imitate, and assigning a role via a system/role prompt sets a consistent voice (indexes 0 and 1). Raising creativity, giving only general descriptions, and letting the structure vary per ticket all work against the goal of consistency (indexes 2, 3, and 4).
referenceText: Anthropic — Use examples (multishot prompting)

stem: A marketing manager asks Claude for the current market-share statistics of three competitors and wants to put the numbers directly into a board deck. What is the most responsible way to handle Claude's response before using it?
options: Ask Claude to cite its sources, then independently verify each statistic against the original reports before using them.
Paste the numbers straight into the deck, since Claude is designed to give factual answers.
Re-run the same prompt twice; if the numbers match, treat them as confirmed.
Add more detail to the prompt so Claude is compelled to be accurate.
rationale: Claude can generate plausible-sounding but incorrect figures, so specific statistics should be verified against authoritative sources before being relied upon; asking for sources and checking them is the recommended practice (index 0). Pasting numbers directly ignores hallucination risk (index 1). Re-running can reproduce the same wrong answer, so agreement is not verification (index 2). Adding detail improves relevance but does not guarantee factual accuracy (index 3).
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center — How up to date is Claude's training data

stem: An HR coordinator wants to use Claude to help draft standard onboarding emails. Which practices best support responsible handling of sensitive data? (Select 2.)
options: Remove or anonymize employees' personal identifiers before pasting information into the chat.
Review the organization's data-handling policies to confirm what information is appropriate to share with Claude.
Include full national ID numbers so the emails are as personalized as possible.
Turn off the review step once a template is approved to speed up sending.
Share whatever detail is convenient, since conversations are private by default.
rationale: Minimizing and anonymizing sensitive personal data before sharing it, and following your organization's data-handling policies, are core responsible-use practices (indexes 0 and 1). Including full national ID numbers unnecessarily exposes sensitive data (index 2). Removing the review step eliminates a key safeguard (index 3). Assuming any data is safe because chats are private misrepresents privacy and encourages oversharing (index 4).
referenceText: Anthropic Privacy Policy — how Anthropic handles your data

stem: A consultant reminds Claude in every new chat that responses must use British spelling, a formal tone, and reference the firm's style guide. Which Claude feature is designed to apply these preferences automatically across related conversations?
options: Setting up custom instructions and using a Project with the style guide as provided knowledge so preferences apply consistently.
Saving the reminder as a reusable text snippet and pasting it into each new chat.
Including the full style guide in every message so nothing is missed.
Turning off chat history so each conversation starts identically.
rationale: Custom instructions let you specify persistent preferences like tone and spelling, and Projects let you attach provided knowledge such as a style guide so Claude applies them across related conversations (index 0). Pasting a snippet or the full guide each time is manual and error-prone, not automatic (indexes 1 and 2). Turning off chat history removes context rather than applying consistent preferences (index 3).
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center — What are Projects

stem: A user needs to quickly summarize dozens of short, routine customer emails throughout the day and cares most about speed and lower cost, not deep reasoning. Which model-selection approach is most appropriate?
options: Choose a faster, lighter model suited to simple high-volume tasks, reserving the most capable model for complex work.
Use the most capable, highest-reasoning model for every email to ensure maximum quality.
Batch all the emails into a single prompt for the largest model so it processes them together.
Alternate between models on each email to balance the workload.
rationale: Matching the model to the task is key: a faster, lighter model suits simple, high-volume work like routine summaries, while the most capable model is best reserved for complex reasoning (index 0). Always using the highest-end model wastes speed and cost on simple tasks (index 1). Batching into the largest model still uses the most expensive, slower option against the user's priorities (index 2). Alternating arbitrarily does not align capability with need (index 3).
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center — What models does Claude offer

stem: A user is not getting useful results from a vague prompt. Which techniques are most likely to improve the quality of Claude's output? (Select 2.)
options: Give clear, direct instructions that state exactly what you want and the desired format.
Provide a concrete example of the kind of output you want.
Keep the prompt broad and open so Claude can interpret each request flexibly.
Add a firm instruction that the answer must be perfect and contain no errors.
Send the identical one-line request several times to reinforce it.
rationale: Being clear and direct about the task and desired format, and providing an example of the output you want, are proven techniques that improve results (indexes 0 and 1). Deliberate broadness gives Claude less to work with and worsens output (index 2). Demanding perfection does not change capability or reduce errors (index 3). Repeating the same vague line adds no useful information (index 4).
referenceText: Anthropic — Be clear and direct (prompt engineering)

stem: A financial analyst plans to use Claude to draft investment recommendations that clients will act on. Which governance practice is most important before those recommendations are shared?
options: Have a qualified human review and validate the recommendations before they are shared.
Add a note that the advice is AI-generated and send it without further review.
Route the draft through a second Claude conversation for review instead of a human.
Ask Claude to confirm in writing that its recommendations are correct, then send them.
rationale: Meaningful human review is essential for high-stakes, consequential outputs like investment advice; a qualified person should validate the work before it is acted upon (index 0). A disclaimer does not substitute for oversight (index 1). A second model pass is still automated and not the human judgment required for consequential decisions (index 2). A model's self-confirmation does not guarantee correctness (index 3).
referenceText: Anthropic Usage Policy — appropriate use and human oversight

stem: A marketing manager asks Claude to "write something about our product" and is unhappy with the vague, generic result. According to Anthropic's guidance on prompting, what is the single most effective first change to improve the output?
options: Give Claude clear, specific direction — including the audience, purpose, desired length, and tone.
Repeat the same prompt several times until Claude produces a better version by chance.
Switch to a different Claude model, since prompt wording rarely affects quality.
Tell Claude to "be creative and detailed" without adding any other specifics.
rationale: Anthropic's prompting guidance emphasizes treating Claude like a new contractor with no context: give specific instructions about audience, purpose, format, length, and tone (index 0). Repeating the same vague prompt adds no information (index 1). Changing models does not fix an under-specified prompt; clarity is the primary lever (index 2). "Be creative and detailed" is still vague and gives nothing concrete to work from (index 3).
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center — Introduction to prompt design

stem: An analyst uses Claude to summarize a market report, and the summary includes a specific statistic attributed to a study the analyst has never seen. The number is about to go into a client deliverable. What is the most responsible next step?
options: Independently confirm the statistic and verify that the cited study exists and actually supports the claim
Trust the statistic, since Claude provided a named citation alongside it
Ask Claude to restate the statistic, and treat it as confirmed if the number matches
Regenerate the summary with a different prompt and use whichever statistic appears in both versions
rationale: Claude can produce plausible-sounding but incorrect facts or citations (hallucinations), so Anthropic advises against treating Claude as a single source of truth and recommends checking cited sources against the original material before relying on high-stakes output. A citation appearing in the output does not guarantee the source exists or supports the claim, so trusting it is unsafe. Asking Claude to restate the number only tests self-consistency, not accuracy, and comparing two generations likewise confirms only agreement between outputs, not correctness. Independent verification against the primary source is the recommended practice.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center — Claude is providing incorrect or misleading responses. What's going on?

stem: A consultant wants a Claude Project to consistently draft client emails in their firm's voice and reference the firm's standard service descriptions, without re-explaining context in every chat. Which two configuration steps best support this? (Select 2.)
options: Add the firm's tone, formatting rules, and standard sign-off as the Project's custom instructions
Upload the firm's service descriptions and style guide to the Project's knowledge so Claude can reference them
Paste the firm's full client contact database into the Project so every draft has recipient details on hand
Begin each email in a new chat with no shared context so responses stay unbiased
Provide a single one-line request and let Claude infer the firm's voice on its own
rationale: Projects let users set project (custom) instructions that persistently guide tone and format, and add documents to project knowledge that Claude can reference across every chat in the Project, which is exactly what maintaining a consistent voice and reusing standard service descriptions requires. Pasting a full client contact database violates data-minimization guidance and is unnecessary for capturing voice. Starting a fresh context-free chat for each email defeats the purpose of a Project, and expecting Claude to infer a specific firm voice from one line contradicts the guidance to give clear, specific instructions.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center — What are Projects?

stem: An HR generalist wants to use Claude to help summarize employee performance notes ahead of review meetings. Which approach best reflects Anthropic's guidance on privacy and responsible use?
options: Remove or generalize identifying and sensitive details before sharing, and have a person review the summary before it is used
Paste full names, ID numbers, and health details so the summary captures every relevant fact
Share the raw notes as-is, since Consumer chats are never viewed by anyone at Anthropic under any circumstances
Let Claude draft the performance conclusions and act on them directly to keep the review process efficient
rationale: Anthropic's guidance encourages minimizing the sensitive and personally identifying information you share and keeping a human in the loop for consequential, people-impacting decisions such as HR matters. Pasting full identifiers and health details is the opposite of data minimization. The claim that chats are never viewed under any circumstances is false: by default Anthropic staff do not view conversations, but limited review can occur, for example to enforce the Usage Policy or for safety, so it is not a safe assumption. Removing human review from a high-stakes HR decision is also inappropriate.
referenceText: Anthropic Help Center — Inputting sensitive data: who can view my conversations?
